segments.s159-g33: نشستن‌گه is a poetic compound meaning 'place of sitting' or 'seat'; here it stands before مجلس, which specifies whose seat it is ('the seat of the assembly/court'). The conjunction و links this whole nominal phrase to میگسار (the next noun).
segments.s159-g34: همان here functions as 'likewise' or 'the same' introducing a parallel list: باز و شاهین are two birds (falcon/hawk) joined by و, and کار شکار is a noun‑noun phrase ('the work/art of hunting'), with کار modified by شکار (no ezâfe, a poetic juxtaposition).
segments.s159-g35: ز is the poetic preposition 'of/about' (repeated for rhythm) introducing paired topics: داد 'justice' and بیداد 'injustice/oppression', then تخت 'throne' and کلاه 'hat/helmet' are coordinated by و. The repetition of ز gives a list-like, emphatic cadence.
segments.s159-g36: سخن گفتن is the verbal nominal phrase 'to speak' or 'speaking'; what follows are its objects/topics: رزم 'war/battle' and then راندن سپاه is an infinitival noun phrase 'the leading/driving of an army' (راندن used as a noun 'leading/commanding'). The و coordinates the two topics.
segments.s159-g37: هنرها is the direct object 'the arts/skills'. بیاموختش is a past verb with the enclitic -ش ('him/it') meaning 'he learned them' (in classical usage the form can also mean 'they taught him', but here context shows he acquired the arts). سر به سر is an idiomatic adverbial phrase meaning 'one and all' or 'completely/throughout'.
segments.s159-g38: بسی here functions adverbially 'much/many'; رنج برداشت is an idiom 'he bore/underwent suffering' where برداشت (literally 'took') is used idiomatically 'to endure'. و links the next clause آمد به بر, where آمد is 'he came' and به بر means 'to (the) presence / to the court' (بر here 'toward/to').
segments.s159-g39: سیاوش is the subject (proper name). چنان شد که is a fixed construction 'became such that...' (چنان = 'such/so', شد = past 'became'), and که introduces the subordinate clause that follows. اندر جهان is a locative phrase 'in the world'.
segments.s159-g40: به مانند او is a simile 'like him' (به همراه ساختار به مانند). کس نبود is the negative existential 'there was no one' (کس here, with نبود, yields 'no one existed'). از مهان means 'of/from the guests' (i.e. 'among the guests'); the preposition از marks the set from which the absence is asserted.
segments.s159-g89: نخست آفرین کرد و بردش نماز — 'نخست' (initial adverb) comes first, setting the sequence. 'آفرین کرد' is a light-verb construction where آفرین (praise/exclamation) is the object/phrase and کرد is the past tense of کردن, 'he made/offered praise' (poetic usage where 'to praise' is rendered by 'آفرین کردن'). The conjunction و links this clause to what follows; 'بردش نماز' is idiomatic/archaic: برد (past of برداشتن or بردن) plus the enclitic -ش (third-person object 'him/it') with نماز as its object — here meaning 'he took up/performed the prayer' (i.e., he engaged in the ritual prayer).
segments.s159-g90: زمانی همی گفت با خاک راز — زمانی here means 'for a while' (a temporal duration). همی is the archaic/progressive particle often marking a repeated or continued action (here: 'kept / for a while'). گفت in context is the verb 'he said/spoke' (the provided lemma senses list shows a noun sense; grammatically this is the past verb). با introduces the companion/recipient 'خاک' (the dust/earth), and راز is the content, 'a secret' — 'گفت با خاک راز' = 'he spoke his secret to the dust' (i.e., he confided in the earth).
segments.s159-g91: وزان پس بیامد بر شهریار — وزان (poetic compound 'و + زآن') means 'and from that / after that', followed by پس 'then/afterwards'. بیامد is the simple past 'he came'. بر + شهریار means 'before/to the king' (بر here marks direction/toward or presence before someone). The whole segment sets the movement toward the sovereign.
segments.s159-g92: سپهبد گرفتش سر اندر کنار — سپهبد is the agent (a military title). گرفتش is گرفت + enclitic -ش (he seized him); گرفت is past of گرفتن. سر is the direct object 'head' (anatomical). اندر کنار is a locative phrase 'at the side / beside (him)' where اندر is a poetic preposition 'in/at' and کنار 'side/beside'. The clause means the spahbed seized him and set/placed his head beside (the king).
segments.s159-g93: شگفتی ز دیدار او خیره ماند — شگفتی here functions as a noun meaning 'astonishment' or 'amazement'. ز is the poetic preposition 'from/because of' and دیدار او 'his sight / seeing him'. خیره ماند is a compound predication: خیره (adjective/participle 'dazed, staring, astonished') plus ماند (past of ماندن 'to remain'), so 'remained astonished' or 'stood transfixed.'
segments.s159-g94: بروبر همی نام یزدان بخواند — بروبر is a compound adverb 'face-to-face / directly'. همی again marks a continuous or iterative action ('kept on / repeatedly'). نام یزدان is 'the name of God' (نام = name, یزدان = God). بخواند is the past/present-archaic-form of خواندن used here to mean 'he uttered/recited/called (the name)'; together: 'he repeatedly recited/called the name of God directly.'
segments.s159-g95: بدان اندکی سال و چندان خرد — بدان is a classical locative/temporal phrase 'in that / to that (time/condition)' (orthographically b + دان here); اندکی سال means 'a few years' (اندکی = a little, سال = years). و links to چندان خرد: چندان ('to that extent/so much') + خرد ('wisdom') yields 'so much wisdom'—the line states a short span of years and that amount of wisdom.
segments.s159-g96: که گفتی روانش خرد پرورد — که introduces a clause 'which / that' (here linking to what was just described). گفتی is 2nd-person past 'you said' (the provided senses list shows noun senses; grammatically it is a verb 'you said'). روانش is روان + enclitic -ش 'his soul/spirit'. خرد پرورد is a verbal/adjectival phrase formed from پرورد (past stem of پروردن 'to nurture') with خرد as the nurturing agent/object: 'nurtured by wisdom' or 'wisdom nurtured his spirit.' The line repeats 'as you said' (گفتی) and states the result.
segments.s159-g73: بفرمود is a third-person past (honorific/polite) verb meaning 'he commanded/said.' تا introduces a subordinate purpose/result clause ('so that' / 'that ...'), and با + noun phrases (با سپه گیو و طوس) marks accompaniment: 'with the army, Giv and Tous.' The proper names follow the noun they accompany, a common poetic ordering.
segments.s159-g74: برفتند is the simple past, 3rd person plural 'they departed/they went' and is the main verb of the subordinate clause introduced by تا. با governs the instruments/companions that follow ('with'), and نای رویین و کوس lists instruments: نای (flute) + رویین (iron/steel, here an adjective) + کوس (war-drum). Note the poetic noun–adjective ordering (نای رویین rather than رویین نای).
segments.s159-g75: همه is a universal quantifier 'all/everyone' modifying نامداران (plural 'the famed/renowned ones'). شدند is the past copular/lexical verb 'became/turned into' used here with انجمن as the predicate, so literally 'all the famed became an assembly' = 'all gathered.'
segments.s159-g76: چو here means 'such as/like' introducing examples. گرگین is a proper name (Gorgin) and و links it to خراد; خراد is used as a name/epithet derived from the adjective 'wise' and is followed by لشکرشکن, an epithet 'army‑breaker' that attributes great military prowess. The epithets follow the names in this poetic order.
segments.s159-g77: پذیره is a noun 'reception/welcoming' used here as the object/event connected to the action; برفتند is the past 3rd‑person plural 'they went/departed.' یکسر is an adverb 'all at once/entirely' and ز جای is a prepositional phrase 'from their places' (ز = 'from', جای = 'place'). The sequence expresses that they immediately left their seats to attend.
segments.s159-g78: به نزد is a prepositional frame 'to the presence of / to' marking the destination. سیاووش is the proper name (Siawush). فرخنده رای is a compound epithet: فرخنده 'auspicious/fortunate' modifies رای 'counsel/opinion' (here 'of auspicious counsel'), with the adjective following the noun in the compact poetic phrase.
segments.s159-g79: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when.' دیدند is past 3rd person plural 'they saw.' گردان is the noun 'troop' (direct object). گو پور شاه functions as a name/nominal chain 'Goupur, son of the king' (پور 'son' linking to شاه 'king'); in classical poetic style name elements and kinship words are placed after the noun they qualify.
segments.s159-g80: خروش آمد is an idiomatic intransitive phrase 'a cry/shout arose' (خروش 'cry/uproar' + آمد 'came'). و links the two clauses, and برگشادند راه is a transitive clause 'they opened/cleared the way' with برگشادند (past 3rd plural 'they opened') followed by راه ('the way/path').
segments.s159-g193: چو is the temporal/conditional conjunction 'when' introducing a subordinate clause; تو شاه is the emphatic address 'you, (as) king' with the title following the pronoun (poetic word order). ننهاد is the past/preterite form of نهاد/نهادن ‘to place, to set’ and takes بر + noun for the place, so ننهاد بر سر کلاه = 'placed upon the head the hat/crown'.
segments.s159-g194: به introduces an adverbial phrase here meaning 'with/in' (adverbial use) modifying how the crown was set; خوبی و دانش are coordinated nouns 'goodness and knowledge' and the second به repeats the same adverbial marker to attach آیین و راه 'ritual and way/method' in parallel — a rhetorical repetition common in Ferdowsi.
segments.s159-g195: مرا is the object 'me' (here split in the data as م + را); ساز is used imperatively or causative 'make/appoint' governed by the verb, so موبدان ساز = 'make (me) mōbedān / appoint (priests for me)'. با + noun phrase ('با بخردان') means 'with the wise' — با marks accompaniment. The poetic segmentation/annotation splits the clitic, so the provided 'م' senses don't match the pronoun and are marked below as unknown.
segments.s159-g196: This is a coordinated list of companions or appointees: بزرگان 'the great/elders' and کارآزموده 'the experienced' modify the final noun ردان. و links the adjectives/phrases. The final ردان is an old/obscure word in this annotation; grammatically it is the head noun of the coordinated phrase.
segments.s159-g197: دگر begins a new alternative 'another / on the other hand'. A simple enumerative noun phrase follows: نیزه و گرز و تیر و کمان listing weapons. Word order is the usual Persian coordination with و between items.
segments.s159-g198: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause 'which/that'; چون here functions as 'when/as' introducing a temporal clause. پیچم is 1st-person singular present/subjunctive form of پیچیدن 'to turn, to whirl' (poetic; -م marks the verb as the speaker's action). اندر صف = 'in/among the line' where صف here means battle-line/formation; بدگمان is an adjective meaning 'suspicious' or 'wary', describing those affected by the action.
segments.s159-g199: دگرگاه is an adverbial/time expression 'at another time'. شاهان is a plural noun 'kings' coordinated with آیین بار ('ceremonial pomp' or 'ceremonial occasion'); بار here combines with آیین to mean an occasion or festal pomp. The phrase is a topical/nominal listing; the copular/existential verb is omitted (typical in poetry).
segments.s159-g200: دگر here means 'again' or 'anew' and introduces a resumed series of nouns (بزم, رزم, می‌و, میگسار) linked by و. There is no explicit verb — the state 'there were' or 'came' is omitted for metrical and poetic brevity. میگسار is 'cupbearer/wine-companion'; می‌و in this poetic context refers to wine/banquet-sense (lexical entry provided does not list 'wine', so note below).
segments.s159-g25: بسی مهتر اندیشه کرد اندر آن is a compact clause: بسی (an adverb) modifies مهتر (chief/elder) to indicate a large number or intensity; مهتر is the noun 'chief(s)'. اندیشه کرد is the past-tense verbal phrase 'thought/pondered' (کرد is the past of کردن). اندر آن is a prepositional phrase 'about that / concerning it' (اندر = 'in, about'; آن = 'that'). Word order is Subject–Object–Verb-phrase typical of classical Persian: the group doing the thinking (مهتر) is followed by the verb کردن and then the locative/oblique phrase اندر آن.
segments.s159-g26: نیامد همی بر دلش بر گران is a compressed predication about the effect on 'him': نیامد is the negative past 'did not come / did not occur'; همی is a classical poetic adverb often marking continuity or emphasis (here strengthening the negative); بر دلش is the prepositional phrase 'upon his heart' (بر = on/upon + دلش = 'his heart' with enclitic ش); بر گران is a short predicate 'heavy upon' where the second بر is the preposition and گران is the adjective 'heavy'. Put together: 'the thought did not come to his heart, (for) it was heavy upon him.'
segments.s159-g27: به رستم سپردش دل و دیده را is built around the dative prepositional phrase به رستم 'to/for Rostam' followed by the past verb سپردش (سپردن 'to entrust' with the pronominal/enclitic -ش referring to the thing/person entrusted). دل و دیده را is the definite object 'the heart and the sight' marked by the object marker را. The implicit subject (he/they) is omitted as is normal in Persian; the enclitic on سپردش indexes the object, while را explicitly flags it as definite/emphatic.
segments.s159-g28: جهانجوی گرد پسندیده را is an appositive phrase describing Rostam. جهانجوی ('world-seeker' = ambitious/one who seeks fame) is a compound epithet. گرد here functions as a verbal/adjectival element 'going about' (so: 'one who goes about seeking the world'), and پسندیده 'chosen/favored' is another epithet; the final را again marks the whole phrase as definite/appositive. In short, these words are stacked epithets after the main clause to qualify Rostam.
segments.s159-g29: تهمتن ببردش به زابلستان names the agent (تهمتن, an epithet of Rostam) followed by ببردش, a past/preterite-form verb 'took him' (here transitive; the -ش may be an enclitic object). به زابلستان is the destination prepositional phrase 'to Zabulistan'. The subject is explicit (تهمتن) and the object is indexed by the enclitic on the verb and/or by context.
segments.s159-g30: نشستن‌گهش ساخت در گلستان: نشستن‌گهش is a possessive noun 'his seat/place' (نشستن‌گاه + enclitic ش = 'his seat'); ساخت is the past tense 'made/built' (used here as a verb); در گلستان is a locative 'in the rose-garden'. Classical Persian often places the object/prepositional phrase before the verb, and the subject is omitted when clear from context.
segments.s159-g31: سواری و تیر و کمان و کمند is a simple coordinate list of martial/equestrian equipment and personnel: سواری 'riders/horsemen', تیر 'arrows', کمان 'bows', کمند 'lassos/snare'. The repeated و = 'and' links the list items; this catalogue style is common in epic poetry.
segments.s159-g32: عنان و رکیب و چه و چون و چند continues the list: عنان 'reins/bridle', رکیب 'companion/attendant' (here a mounted companion or retainer), followed by the idiomatic trio چه و چون و چند 'what and how and how many' meaning 'all sorts of other particulars'. These interrogative words are used elliptically to mean 'various other things' rather than literal questions.
segments.s159-g161: همه (all) quantifies the noun phrase; روی پوشیدگان را is the definite/marked object phrase 'the faces of the veiled ones' (روی = face, پوشیدگان = the veiled/covered), with را as the object/definiteness marker. ز مهر is a poetic prepositional phrase placed after the object meaning 'from/because of love' (ز = archaic poetic form of از, مهر = love/affection).
segments.s159-g162: پر ... است is the predicate: پر 'full (of)' is used predicatively with an implied copula -ست (here merged onto دلست). The clause reads 'is full of the blood of the heart' (پر ازخون دلست = پر از خونِ دل است), then joined by و to a parallel phrase 'and full of the water of the face' (پر از آب چهر) — آب چهر = tears/face-water. Word order is poetic and the copula is fused to دل (دلست).
segments.s159-g163: نمازش is 'her/their prayer' (نماز + possessive -ش). برند is a present/plural verbal form meaning 'they carry/take' (بردن) used here in a narrative/habitual sense. و links to نثار آورند, where نثار 'offering/dedication' is the object and آورند 'they bring' is a present/plural verb. The verbs are in the classical/polite narrative present (—ند).
segments.s159-g164: درخت پرستش is a genitive/compound nominal phrase 'the tree of worship' (درخت = tree, پرستش = worship). به بار آوردند uses the light-verb idiom 'به بار آوردن' = 'to cause to bear fruit / to make fruitful'; آورند is again the 3rd-person plural present 'they bring/cause'. The whole means 'they cause the tree of worship to bear (fruit)', i.e. they foster devotion.
segments.s159-g165: بدو گفت شاه is inverted classical word order: بدو (to him) + گفت (said) + شاه (the king) = 'the king said to him.' این سخن در خورست is a predicate clause: این سخن 'this speech/these words' and در خورست is an archaic adjective phrase meaning 'is fitting/suitable' (در خور + -ست copula fused). Note the copula -ست is attached to خور here.
segments.s159-g166: برو is the imperative 'go.' بر here is the preposition 'upon/on' followed by the clitic ترا (given in the text as ت + را) meaning 'you' in oblique case; together 'بر ترا' = 'upon you.' مهر صد مادرست is a verbless nominal clause with final copula -ست: 'the love of a hundred mothers it is' → 'a hundred mothers' love rests on you.' The order is poetic (copula at end).
segments.s159-g167: سپهبد is the subject ('the spahbed / general'). سیاووش را is the direct object with را (Siavash). خواند here means 'called/summoned' (past form) — in classical narrative the past tense is used for reporting. و گفت follows: 'and said' (introducing the reported clause).
segments.s159-g168: که introduces the content clause 'that ...'. خون و رگ و مهر is a triad 'blood, r(egg)/lineage, and love' (رگ here can mean lineage/heritage in this context). نتوان is the negative of توانستن 'cannot' (present/impersonal form), and نهفت is used as 'be hidden'/'be concealed' (past/participial form functioning with نتوان to mean 'cannot be hidden'). Together: 'that blood, lineage, and love cannot be concealed.'
segments.s159-g361: بدانست is a past-tense verb (3rd person singular) meaning 'he realized/understood' (ب- + دانست). کان here is an archaic particle meaning 'that/as before' (used to point to the preceding idea) and is followed by نیز 'also', so کان نیز together mark 'that also / this too'. گفتار اوست is a nominal clause: گفتار 'speech' (head noun) + او 'his/her' + ست (poetic copula 'is') at the end, giving 'this too is his speech.'
segments.s159-g362: همی is a classical/poetic iterative marker meaning 'kept on / continuously' (here 'kept tearing'). ز + و here represent the preposition 'از' (poetic z) + an enclitic object ('-u' = 'him/her'), so together mean 'from him' (the segmentation lists them separately). بدرید is past tense 'tore' (here iterative/continuous with همی, implying repeated action, likely 3rd-person plural). بر تنش پوست is a prepositional-phrase object: بر 'off/upon' + تنش 'his body' (تن + enclitic ش = 'his') + پوست 'skin', literally 'the skin off his body.'
segments.s159-g81: This noun phrase names the attendant and gives two accompanying instruments/qualities with the preposition با ('with'). مجمر and بوی خوش are coordinated by و; بوی خوش is a noun + adjective (smell + pleasant) in the normal Persian order. The whole phrase functions as a topical/agentive phrase describing who performs the action that follows.
segments.s159-g82: This clause contains the action: نظاره ('a gaze/looking') followed by the participial periphrasis دست کرده ('having put/handed') plus a directional/goal phrase به کش. Here نظاره functions as the action of watching/looking; دست کرده is a perfect participle used with the implied subject to mean 'he put (his) hand'. The prepositional element به introduces the target (toward the sword/sheath). Note: some classical word forms (e.g. برو as a contraction) are idiomatic; کش here refers to the act/target of drawing (i.e. the sword/scabbard) rather than the literal senses in the lemma list.
segments.s159-g83: بهر is a prepositional/locative marker ('at/to') introducing the location کنج در ('corner of the gate'); سیصد is the numeral (here 'three hundred') placed before the verb. استاده بود is the past-tense periphrasis meaning 'stood' / 'were standing' (بود is the past copula). The clause therefore gives the number and location of those who stood.
segments.s159-g84: میان در is a locative phrase 'in the middle of the doorway'; the proper name سیاووش is the subject and آزاده بود is a predicate: آزاده is an adjectival predicate ('noble/free-hearted') and بود is the past copula 'was'. Word order is typical Persian: locative phrase — subject — predicate.
segments.s159-g85: بسی here is an archaic intensifier meaning 'very many' or 'much' and modifies the direct objects زر و گوهر ('gold and jewels'). برافشاندند is a transitive past-tense verb (3rd person plural) meaning 'they scattered' / 'they showered' — the verb comes at the end as is standard in Persian.
segments.s159-g86: سراسر ('throughout') is an adverbial modifier; همه is an emphatic/plural subject ('all/everyone'); آفرین is an interjection ('bravo' / acclamation) and خواندند is the past-tense verb 'they uttered/called' (here 'they cried out'). The sequence means that everyone throughout cried out 'afarin'.
segments.s159-g87: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'; کاووس را uses the object marker را to mark Kaavus as the person perceived, followed by the past verb دید ('saw'). بر تخت عاج is a locative prepositional phrase ('upon the ivory throne') with بر + noun; عاج (ivory) modifies تخت (throne). The clause sets the scene for the observer's perception.
segments.s159-g88: ز is the poetic genitive/partitive 'of/from' introducing the material/quality (ز یاقوت 'of ruby'); رخشنده is an adjective 'radiant/bright' modifying the implied jewel; بر سرش is the locative 'on his head' (بر + سر + the enclitic ش 'his'); تاج is the head noun 'crown'. The phrase describes the crown decorating Kaavus's head.
segments.s159-g65: جهان گشته is a nominal phrase where گشته (perfect participle / “has become”) predicates about جهان (“the world”). پر is a prepositional/adverbial modifier meaning “full (of)” that directly precedes the nouns it fills, so پر شادی و خواسته = “full of joy and wishes.” The phrase as a whole = “the world had become full of joy and desires.”
segments.s159-g66: در و بام is a coordinated noun phrase “doors and roofs,” and هر برزن is a genitive/attributive phrase “of every barzan (village/square).” آراسته is a past participle used predicatively (“were adorned”). Word order here is locative/possessive phrase followed by predicate, a common poetic order: “the doors and roofs of every village were adorned.”
segments.s159-g67: به زیر پی is a locative phrase “beneath the foot/hoof,” where به + زیر marks position under something. پی here means “foot/hoof.” تازی اسپان is a noun phrase “Arabian horses” modifying the location, and درم is the head noun “dirham(s).” The whole unit reads as a noun phrase: “dirhams under the hooves of the Arabian horses.”
segments.s159-g68: به ایران is a locative phrase “in/within Iran.” نبودند is the past negative plural of بودن (“there were not”); here the verb precedes the nominal phrase یک تن دژم (a single sullen person), a common existential word order in Persian (locative/verb/thing). The clause means “in Iran there was not a single sullen person.”
segments.s159-g69: همه modifies یال اسپ to mean “all the manes of the horses.” از کران تا کران is an idiomatic range “from edge to edge / end to end” where از … تا marks the span. The phrase as a whole sets the extent: “all the horses’ manes, from end to end.”
segments.s159-g70: براندوده is a past/adjectival form describing how the manes were treated (here meaning ‘spread/smeared/strewn’ in context); مشک, می‌و, زعفران are the items involved. The conjunction و links them. The whole phrase describes the manes being covered with musk, mi(ye)/fruit (here poetically wine/fruit), and saffron.
segments.s159-g71: چو آمد is a temporal subordinate (“when it came”). به کاووس شاه marks the recipient/target “to Kavus, the king” with به plus the name and title. آگهی is the noun “news/information” and functions as the incoming item. Word order places the temporal clause first, then recipient and news (“when news came to King Kavus”).
segments.s159-g72: که introduces the content clause (“that …”). آمد is the past tense “came,” and سیاووش is the subject/name. با فرهی is a prepositional phrase of accompaniment/manner (“with nobility/honour”); فرهی is an adjectival/nominal word describing his manner. The clause means “that Siavush came with honour.”
segments.s159-g49: گو شیردل کار او را بساخت — 'گو' is a literary/exclamatory particle introducing the clause; 'شیردل' is an epithet, a noun phrase meaning “the lion‑hearted (one).” Word order is theme + object + verb: 'کار او را' (کار = affair, او = his, را = definite/direct‑object marker) precedes the past tense verb 'بساخت' (classical past 'he made/arranged'). The object marker را attaches to 'او'+object to indicate the definite object ('his affair').
segments.s159-g50: فرستادگان را ز هر سو بتاخت — 'فرستادگان را' = 'the messengers' with را marking them as the definite object; the preposition 'ز' (poetic 'from') introduces the source/direction 'هر سو' ('every side'). The verb 'بتاخت' is a classical narrative past (third person) meaning 'they charged/they rode swiftly'; the clause thus says he dispatched messengers who galloped from every quarter.
segments.s159-g51: ز اسپ و پرستنده و سیم و زر — the preposition 'ز' (from/of) begins a rhythmic list of valuables. Items are coordinated with و (and): 'اسپ' (steeds), 'پرستنده' (attendants/devotees/servants here), 'سیم' (silver) and 'زر' (gold). In classical Persian poetry such nominal lists often omit the verb and rely on context (the procuring/sending action appears elsewhere).
segments.s159-g52: ز مهر و ز تخت و کلاه و کمر — another coordinated list introduced by repeated 'ز' for rhythm: 'مهر' (here meaning favour/affection/gifts), 'تخت' (throne), 'کلاه' (helmet/hat) and 'کمر' (belt/girdle). The repeated 'ز' is a poetic device enumerating categories of valuables; again the verb is implied from surrounding lines.
segments.s159-g53: ز پوشیدنی هم ز گستردنی — 'ز' (from/of) again introduces items; 'پوشیدنی' is a noun/adjective meaning 'clothing/garments' (literally 'things to be worn'). 'هم' adds 'also' (inclusive). 'گستردنی' literally 'things to be spread' (e.g. carpets, rugs, coverings) functions as a noun in this list; poetic nominalization with the -نی ending is common.
segments.s159-g54: ز هر سو بیاورد آوردنی — 'ز هر سو' = 'from every side/quarter' (ز = from, هر سو = every direction). The verb 'بیاورد' (present/subjunctive/imperative form in modern Persian but here used as a classical infinitive/optative nuance) combined with the adjectival/nominal suffix 'آوردنی' ('things to be brought' or 'bringable items') turns the phrase into 'things he brought from every quarter.' In context the rhythm and repetition of verbal/adjectival endings is a poetic device.
segments.s159-g55: ازین هر چه در گنج رستم نبود — 'ازین' (archaic fused form of 'از این') = 'of these/from these'; 'هر چه' = 'whatever' or 'all that' (indefinite relative). 'در گنج رستم' = 'in Rostam's treasury' (در = in, گنج = treasure, رستم = name). 'نبود' is the past negative 'was not' (third person past), so the clause means 'whatever of these was not in Rostam's treasure.'
segments.s159-g56: ز گیتی فرستاد و آورد زود — 'ز گیتی' = 'from/across the world' (poetic use of گیتی = earth/world). The verbs 'فرستاد و آورد' are two past actions in sequence: 'he sent (agents) and brought (the things)'; 'زود' is an adverb meaning 'quickly/soon' modifying the bringing. The clause states he dispatched people throughout the world and quickly brought back what was missing.
segments.s159-g41: چو + time phrase introduces a temporal clause 'when/after ... had passed' (چو = 'when'). یک چند بگذشت is an idiom 'a little while passed' where چند is an indefinite 'some/a while' and بگذشت is the past 'passed' referring to time. و او شد بلند uses شد (past 'became') with the adjective بلند to mean 'he rose / became upright' (شد + adjective = become/assume that state).
segments.s159-g42: سوی marks direction ('toward'); گردن شیر is the noun phrase 'the lion's neck' (noun + possessor). شد here indicates a change of position/state (poetic use meaning 'moved/turned'), and با کمند is an instrumental phrase 'with a lasso' where با denotes the instrument used.
segments.s159-g43: چنین گفت = 'thus he said/spoke' (چنین = 'thus/so'). با + name is used to mark the addressee ('to/with Rostam'), and سرفراز is a postpositive epithet 'the proud/victorious' modifying رستم.
segments.s159-g44: که introduces a relative clause ('who/that'). آمد is past 'came', به دیدار = 'to the meeting/visit (to the presence of)', شاهم is شاه + enclitic -م 'my king' (i.e. 'the king's presence' or 'my king'), and نیاز here means 'supplication/entreaty' describing the manner/purpose of the visit.
segments.s159-g45: بسی is an intensive 'much/very many' modifying رنج 'suffering'; بردی is the past 2nd-person singular of بردن 'to bear/undergo' meaning 'you endured'. و links clauses; دل سوختی is idiomatic ('your heart burned') with سوختی (past) describing grief or anguish.
segments.s159-g46: هنرهای = 'the arts/skills' (plural). شاهانم is شاهان + enclitic -م 'my kings' (possessive), and آموختی is a past second-person form meaning here 'you learned' (context selects 'learn' rather than 'teach'). The phrase is a direct object: 'you learned the skills of my kings.'
segments.s159-g47: پدر is a vocative 'father'. باید اکنون expresses necessity 'it is necessary now'. که introduces a subordinate clause; بیند is a third-person present/subjunctive of دیدن used after باید to express 'that he see / be shown / learn' (here 'may learn/see from me'), and ز من is the source 'from me'.
segments.s159-g48: هنرهای = 'the arts/skills'. آموزش here qualifies هنرها as 'instructional/teaching' (i.e. training or instruction). پیلتن is a place/name that specifies whose instruction is meant: 'the instructional arts of Piltan.' The phrase functions as the content/source that he should 'see/learn from.'
segments.s159-g1: 'بسی برنیامد برین روزگار' is a compact, somewhat archaic clause meaning 'there was not often in these times.' بسی is an adverb ('much/often'); برنیامد is a past negative compound meaning 'did not appear/occur.' بر + ین (here written together as برین) marks the temporal phrase 'in these' (بر = upon/in, ین = form of 'این' = these) and روزگار = 'time/times.' The whole segment sets up a general negative statement about that era.
segments.s159-g2: که introduces a subordinate clause ('that/when'). رنگ is the grammatical subject meaning 'color' or 'hue/beauty.' اندر آمد is a slightly archaic locative + verb construction ('came into' instead of modern در آمد); اندر = 'in/within' and آمد = past 'came.' به marks the target/location ('into/toward') and خرم بهار = 'the joyful/lush spring' (خرم as an adjective modifying بهار). Together: 'that color came into the joyful spring.'
segments.s159-g3: جدا گشت زو کودکی = 'her childhood separated (from her).' جدا is an adverb/adjective 'apart/separate,' گشت is past of گشت 'became/turned (was made),' and زو is the poetic contraction of از + او ('from her'). کودکی is the noun 'childhood.' The clause means her childhood left and she was transformed/matured.
segments.s159-g4: به چهره بسان بت آزری is a comparative phrase describing appearance: به چهره = 'in (her) face/appearance' (به here marks the manner/location), بسان = 'like/as,' and بت آزری = 'the Azari idol' (a poetic image for an exceedingly beautiful face). The phrase says her look became fairy-like, like a famed idol of beauty.
segments.s159-g5: بگفتند با شاه کاووس کی = 'They said to King Kavus: who…?' بگفتند is past 'they said'; با here functions in the older style like 'to' (they addressed) before شاه کاووس (the addressee). کی is the interrogative 'who' (placed at the end as is common in older/poetic Persian).
segments.s159-g6: که begins the relative/subordinate clause ('that/which'). برخوردی here is the 2nd-person past form 'you encountered' (addressing the king) rather than a simple noun; از = 'of/from' introduces source/association, and ماه فرخنده‌پی is a descriptive epithet ('the fortunate-footed moon' — a poetic epithet for a beautiful person). Grammatically this asks 'Who did you encounter, (the one) from/of the fortunate-footed moon?'
segments.s159-g7: یکی is an indefinite determiner meaning 'a certain' placed before the noun phrase. بچه فرخ is a noun (بچه) followed by the adjective فرخ in post‑nominal position (poetic/classical order) meaning 'fortunate child'. آمد پدید is a poetic verb phrase: پدید آمد (to appear) is inverted here to آمد پدید, a past action 'has come forth/appeared.'
segments.s159-g8: کنون is a temporal adverb 'now'. تخت بر ابر is the phrase 'the throne upon the cloud(s)' (تخت = throne). باید + verb expresses necessity; in classical/poetic Persian باید کشید functions as 'one must (do) X' where کشید is the bare past/stem form used to mean 'to draw/bring/bear' (here 'to set/bear the throne onto the clouds') — note the object marker را is omitted (stylistic).
segments.s159-g97: بسی is an adverb meaning 'much/many' here (archaic/poetic); آفرین is a noun/exclamation 'acclamation' (praise). بر is the preposition 'to/toward' used with a recipient, and جهان‌آفرین is a compound noun 'world‑creator' (Creator of the world). Together the phrase is an object/exclamation: 'many acclamations to the Creator of the world.'
segments.s159-g98: بخواند is past (3rd‑person plural in classical style) 'they recited/offered' (here 'recited/said praise'); the coordinating و = 'and' links the clauses. بمالید is likewise past 'they rubbed' (b- prefix + past stem), رخ 'face' is the direct object, بر 'upon/on' marks the surface, and زمین 'earth/ground' is the place. The order is verb + object + locative ('they rubbed face upon the ground').
segments.s159-g99: همی گفت uses همی to mark continual or iterative action ('kept saying'); گفت here is the verb 'said' (the provided senses list did not include the verbal sense). کای is a vocative particle 'O', and کردگار سپهر is a noun phrase 'Maker/Creator of the sky/heavens.' The structure is iterative verb + quoted vocative phrase.
segments.s159-g100: This is a coordinated nominal address: خداوند هوش و خداوند مهر — 'Lord of Intellect and Lord of Love.' خداوند is an honorific/sovereign‑style title here, هوش 'intellect' names the attribute, و 'and' links the two parallel noun phrases, and مهر 'love' is the second attribute. The repetition of خداوند is rhetorical/paratactic.
segments.s159-g101: همه نیکویها 'all the good things/virtues' is the subject phrase. به گیتی is a locative phrase 'in the world' (به + گیتی), ز تست is a poetic contraction equivalent to از توست ('are from you'); note تست here is archaic/contracted and appears as a fused form. The clause asserts source/possession: 'all virtues in the world are from you.'
segments.s159-g102: نیایش 'prayer/worship' is the object; ز فرزند 'from the child/son' gives the source; گیرم is first‑person singular present/subjunctive form 'I take'/'I will take' (classical usage where -م indicates 1st person), and نخست is adverb 'first.' Word order is object + source + verb + adverb, giving 'I will first take worship from the child.'
segments.s159-g103: ز رستم 'from Rostam' functions as the object of inquiry: 'they asked Rostam' (بپرسید is past 'they asked'). The coordinating و = 'and' links the next action. بنواختش contains the b‑prefix + past stem نواخت and the enclitic -ش 'him/it', here meaning 'they played (music) for him' or 'they struck (an instrument) for him.'
segments.s159-g104: بران is a deictic locative 'upon that/thereon', تخت 'throne' names the object, پیروزه is an epithet (here 'turquoise' — a descriptive color/ornament), and بنشاختش is past 'they set/seated him' (b‑prefix + past stem + -ش 'him'). The clause means 'they set him upon that turquoise throne.'
segments.s159-g57: گسی (an indefinite 'someone') + کرد (past of کردن) here means 'caused/made' — together they mean 'someone caused/put (him)'. ازان گونه is a prepositional phrase 'from that kind / in that manner' (گونه = 'manner'). او را is the accusative 'him' (را marks a definite object) and به راه = 'onto the road / on his way.' Word order is somewhat flexible/poetic: verb (کرد) follows the indefinite subject and is followed by the manner and object phrase.
segments.s159-g58: که introduces a result/relative clause ('so that' / 'whereupon'). شد is the past of شدن 'became' and here marks the change of state 'turned/was made to be.' بر is the preposition 'upon/toward' and نظاره سپاه literally 'the army's gaze' (نظاره = sight, سپاه = army) — together: 'the army turned/was turned to gaze upon Siyâvash.' The verb لفظ is placed earlier in the clause by poetic order, but the sense is causal/resultive.
segments.s159-g59: همی + رفت is the habitual/continuous past 'was going / kept going' (همی is the old continuous/iterative marker). با او تهمتن = 'Tahmêtan with him' (با = 'with', او = 'him', تهمتن = proper name/epithet), and به هم ('together') reinforces joint movement. Poetic word order places the companion's name after the pronoun.
segments.s159-g60: بدان is a fused/archaic particle here meaning 'with that / considering that / thereby' (formed from ب + دان) and introduces the following purpose clause; تا marks purpose/result 'so that / in order that.' نباشد is the negative present/subjunctive of بودن 'to be' ('may not be' / 'not remain'). سپهبد دژم is the affected person 'the grim/sullen spahbed.' The clause therefore gives the purpose of accompanying: 'so that the stern spahbed would not remain sullen.'
segments.s159-g61: جهانی here means 'the whole/worldwide' (object/extent) and is placed before the verb. به آیین is an adverbial phrase 'with ceremony / in ritual/with pomp' (به functioning as a preposition). بیاراستند is past tense, 3rd person plural of آراستن with the prefix بی‑, meaning 'they adorned/arrayed (it)'.
segments.s159-g62: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. خشنودی here means 'pleasure/approval' and is the thing desired; نامور modifies it, 'renowned/famed'. خواستند is simple past, 3rd person plural 'they wished/sought.' The sequence gives 'when they sought the famed approval.'
segments.s159-g63: همه زر is 'all the gold' (همه as quantifier before the noun). به عنبر here means 'together with / into amber' (an instrumental/comitative sense). برآمیختند is past tense, 3rd person plural of آمیختن with the prefix بر‑, 'they mingled/combined.'
segments.s159-g64: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' (ز گنبد = 'from the dome'). به سر means 'onto the head / on the top' (به as a directional/prepositional marker). بر is another locative 'upon' reinforcing the motion, همی is the classical progressive marker (ongoing/habitual action) attached before the verb, and ریختند is past plural 'they poured' — together: 'from the dome they were pouring down upon the heads.'
segments.s159-g289: همه نیکویی در جهان بهر تست is a noun phrase plus locative and beneficiary phrase. همه نیکویی = “all (the) goodness” (noun phrase). در جهان is a locative modifier “in the world.” بهر + تست expresses benefit/possession “for you” (بهر = for/to; تست here functions as an archaic/pronominal form meaning ‘(it) is yours/for you’).
segments.s159-g290: ز یزدان بهانه نبایدت جست is a short clause giving a prohibition or negated necessity. ز یزدان = “from/of God” (ز = from/because of, here marking source/agent). بهانه = “an excuse/pretext.” نبایدت جست combines the negative/modal نباید + enclitic -ت (to you) followed by the lexical verb جست (infinitive/short form ‘seek’); literally ‘you ought not to seek’ or ‘you must not seek.’
segments.s159-g291: ز جم و فریدون و هوشنگ شاه is an origin/source phrase introduced by ز = ‘from’ (poetic). The three proper names are coordinated with و and شاه follows as an appositive/title, so the line names those rulers (Jam, Fereydun, Hushang) as kings.
segments.s159-g292: فزونی به گنج و به شمشیر و گاه is a predicative noun phrase describing the domain of increase. فزونی = ‘increase/abundance.’ The repeated به + noun ('به گنج و به شمشیر') lists spheres in which that increase occurred (‘in treasure and in sword/arms’). گاه (‘time/occasion’) is another coordinate element; the clause is elliptical (the copula or verb is omitted, as is common in poetry), so it reads as ‘there was increase in…’.
segments.s159-g293: ز گفتار او شاد شد شهریار: ز گفتار او = ‘from/because of his speech’ (ز = from/because of). شاد شد is the compound verb ‘became happy’ (شاد = happy, شد = became), and شهریار placed after the verb names the person affected (‘the king’). The verb+subject order (شاد شد شهریار) reflects poetic/classical word order where the verb phrase can appear before the explicit subject.
segments.s159-g294: بیاراست ایوان چو خرم بهار: بیاراست is a past/finished-form verb ‘adorned/made beautiful’ (here finite past, third person plural or singular depending on meter/context). ایوان is the direct object ‘the iwan/hall.’ چو introduces a simile ‘like/as,’ and خرم بهار = ‘joyful/lush spring’ modifies how the iwan was adorned (the iwan became like a joyful spring).
segments.s159-g295: می‌و بربط و نای برساختند lists objects produced or set forth: می (wine), بربط (barbat/lute), نای (ney/reed flute). برساختند is a past plural verb ‘they made/put forth/composed.’ The coordinated nouns are direct objects of برساختند; the verb is plural to agree with an implied plural subject (‘they’).
segments.s159-g296: دل از بودنیها بپرداختند has دل as the semantic subject/affected thing (‘the heart’), از بودنیها = ‘from things that are/from worldly beings/earthly existence’ (از marking separation/from). بپرداختند is a past plural verb here meaning ‘they turned away (their hearts), they made the heart depart from…’ — in classical usage پرداختن with از can mean to detach or to withdraw from, so the whole phrase means their hearts were taken away from/ceased to attend to worldly things.
segments.s159-g241: شبستان (shabestān) is the location noun placed before the subject; here it means the palace/inner audience chamber. همه is the subject pronoun/quantifier ‘everyone’ modifying the (implied) people. پیشباز is an adverbial/participial concept ‘to meet/welcome’ that describes the purpose/manner of their coming. آمدند is the 3rd person plural past tense (‘they came’); overall word order is topic (place) + subject + adverbial + verb, typical of Persian narrative.
segments.s159-g242: پر از is the fixed phrase meaning ‘full of’ (پر: ‘full’, followed by the preposition از ‘of’). شادی is a noun ‘joy’ and بزم ساز is a compound meaning the music/instruments of the banquet (بزم ‘feast’ + ساز ‘instrument/melody’). آمدند repeats the 3rd pl. past ‘they came’ and links back to the previous verb to say how they came.
segments.s159-g243: همه جام means ‘every cup’ or ‘all the cups’; جام is the noun ‘cup’. بود is the past copula ‘was’. از کران تا کران is a fixed adverbial phrase ‘from edge to edge / from rim to rim’ where از…تا indicates range (تا here = ‘as far as’). Word order: subject + copula + range adverbial.
segments.s159-g244: پر از again = ‘full of’. مشک ‘musk’ and دینار ‘dinar(s)’ are simple nouns joined by و ‘and’. The second پر before زعفران repeats the ‘full (of)’ construction, here taking زعفران ‘saffron’ as the complement. The whole phrase enumerates what the cups contain.
segments.s159-g245: درم is the noun ‘dirham(s)’. زیر پایش is a locative phrase ‘under his foot’ (زیر = under, پای = foot, ش = enclitic possessive ‘his’). همی is an archaic-frequency particle meaning continuous or iterative action (‘again and again’/‘kept on’). ریختند is the 3rd person plural past of ریختن ‘to pour / to scatter’ (they poured/scattered). The clause thus describes repeated scattering of coins under his feet.
segments.s159-g246: عقیق و زبرجد are two gem names joined by و ‘and’ (عقیق = agate, زبرجد = peridot). برآمیختند is the past 3rd pl. of برآمیختن ‘to mix/mingle together’ (بر- prefix + آمیختن). Grammatically this clause coordinates with the previous action: gems were mixed (in with the coins).
segments.s159-g247: زمین بود places the noun ‘the ground/earth’ before the copula بود ‘was’ (past). در زیر دیبای چین is a locative phrase: در (in) + زیر (under/beneath) + دیبای چین ‘folds of brocade’ (دیبا = brocade; چین = fold). The line states location: the ground lay under the folds of brocade.
segments.s159-g248: پر از = ‘full of’. در here in the text functions as a noun meaning ‘pearls/jewels’ (sense not supplied in the provided list), so it is marked as -1; خوشاب is ‘pleasant drink/sweet wine’, و links items, روی زمین means ‘on the earth / upon the ground’ (روی = on/upon, زمین = earth). Grammatically this continues the descriptive inventory: the earth was filled with pearls and pleasant draughts.
segments.s159-g137: چنین is an adverb 'thus/so' placed at the start for emphasis. خواندندش is a past-tense verb (خواندند, 3rd pl.) with the object enclitic -ش 'him/it' attached, so literally 'they called/named him'; همی is the old/poetic iterative/continuous particle meaning 'always/used to', and پیشتر is an adverb 'earlier/before'. Together they mean 'they used to call him thus before.'
segments.s159-g138: که is the subordinating particle 'that' introducing the content of what was said. خوانی here is best read as a deverbal noun/idea of 'a calling/summons' (from خواندن 'to call/summon') rather than a simple present verb. ورا is a directional/objectal clitic meaning 'thither/there', ماوراء النهر is the compound 'beyond the river (Oxus)', and بر at the end is the archaic placement of the preposition 'to/toward' (the word order is metrically inverted for the verse).
segments.s159-g139: برآمد is a past verb 'arose/occurred' (impersonal here: 'there arose'). برین should be read as بر + ین (an older/dialectal form of the demonstrative 'این'), so 'upon/for this'; thus برآمد برین means 'this also happened/there arose for this'. نیز 'also' modifies the clause and یک روزگار is a temporal noun phrase 'one time / one day'.
segments.s159-g140: چنان بد که is a fixed comparative/result phrase 'so/such ... that' (چنان ... که = 'so ... that'). سودابه is the proper name 'Sūdābeh', and پرنگار is an epithet 'richly adorned / fair-faced' attached to the name; the clause sets up that a very bad/ill time affected Sudabeh.
segments.s159-g141: ز ناگاه is an adverbial phrase meaning 'suddenly' (ز here = 'from' used poetically with ناگاه). روی سیاوش is the object phrase 'Siyavash's face' (noun + name), and بدید is the simple past 'saw' (3rd sing.). Word order is adverb → object → verb, a common poetic inversion.
segments.s159-g142: پراندیشه گشت is a copular/result clause: پراندیشه 'thoughtful/pensive' + گشت 'became' (past of شدن). و links the clauses. دلش is 'her heart' (دل + enclitic ش = her), and بردمید is a past transitive ('carried off/seized') used idiomatically: 'her heart was carried away' = she became enamored; note that بردمید here is passive/affected in sense though morphologically active.
segments.s159-g143: چنان شد که = an impersonal past: چنان (so/thus) + شد (past of شدن 'to become/happen') + که introducing the content clause. گفتی here functions parenthetically as the 2nd-person past verb 'you said' (literary), so the whole phrase means 'it became as you said.' طراز نخ است is a simple nominal clause: طراز (subject) + نخ (predicate nominative) + است (copula).
segments.s159-g144: وگر = conditional 'if/but if'; پیش آتش = prepositional/locative phrase 'before the fire' (پیش + noun). نهاده is a past participle/perfect form 'placed' (used adjectivally/passively), and the clause ends with یخ است 'it is ice' (یخ predicate + است copula). The verb 'is' is explicit rather than omitted.
segments.s159-g353: که here is the relative/conjunction 'that/who', introducing a clause describing someone. گفتار او is a genitival phrase 'his speech/words' (word order: noun + pronoun). مهربانی بود puts the predicate after the subject in past tense: 'was kindness' (بود = past copula). Together: 'that his speech was kindness.'
segments.s159-g354: به جان تو is an idiomatic prepositional phrase 'for your life' or 'for your safety' (به + noun + personal pronoun). بر پاسبانی بود is a verbal phrase formed with the noun پاسبانی 'guarding' plus the copula بود, with بر marking the role 'on/for guarding' — literally 'was on guarding' = 'served as a guard'. The whole clause: 'was a guardian for your life.'
segments.s159-g355: ز here marks cause/agent 'because of' (ز = 'from/because of') and attaches to گفتار او 'his words'. شاد شد is a light verb construction: adjective شاد 'happy' plus شد 'became' = 'became happy'. Word order is topical: subject (سیاوش) + z-phrase + predicate.
segments.s159-g356: نهانش 'his hidden (part/inner self)' is the noun نهان with the clitic -ش; ز here functions as 'from' (source). اندیشه 'thought' is the source from which he is freed; آزاد شد is 'became free' (آزاد + شد). The clause literally: 'his hidden part from thought became free' = 'his inner self was freed from thought/worry.'
segments.s159-g357: به شاه جهان is a dative/goal phrase 'to/for the king of the world' (به + title + apposition). بر ستایش گرفت is a verbal idiom: گرفتن with a noun object (ستایش) and the preposition بر gives the sense 'to take up/begin praising' — گرفت here = 'took up' or 'offered'. Word order places the goal before the verbal phrase.
segments.s159-g358: نوان 'youthful/young' qualifies the agent (understood subject). پیش تختش 'before his throne' uses پیش in the sense 'in the presence of' (پیش + تخت + possessive -ش). نیایش گرفت repeats the pattern گرفتن with the noun 'prayer' to mean 'offered/took up prayer.' The clause: 'the young (one) offered prayer before his throne.'
segments.s159-g359: نهانی is adverbial here 'secretly' (used to qualify the action). ز سودابه indicates the agent/source 'by/from Sudabeh' or 'on Sudabeh's part' (ز = 'from/by'). چاره‌گر is an agentive noun 'one who devises a remedy/contriver' used here to mean someone plotting or devising a solution. Together: 'secretly, from Sudabeh a contriver (was)...' — i.e., 'Sudabeh secretly contrived.'
segments.s159-g360: همی بود is the habitual/continuous copular phrase 'was continually' (همی = 'always/still' + بود 'was'). پیچان is an adjective 'twisting/wily' describing behavior; و links to خسته جگر, a compound describing emotional effect. خسته جگر uses خسته 'weary' + جگر 'heart' (seat of feeling) = 'heart-wearying' or 'wearing out the heart.' The line characterizes ongoing, cunning vexation that exhausts the heart.
segments.s159-g153: دگر روز = 'the next/other day' (دگر here = 'another/next') + روز 'day'. شبگیر is an epithet placed before the name meaning 'one who comes by night / night-visitor'. سودابه is the proper name. رفت is a past-tense verb 'went'; Persian often places time words and epithets before the subject and verb ('the next day, night-visiting Sūdābeh went').
segments.s159-g154: بر شاه ایران = 'toward/to the King of Iran' where بر + (title + place) gives direction/target. خرامید is a past of خرامیدن, 'strode/pranced proudly' describing her manner of approach. تفت is obscure here (likely a poetic/exclamatory or corrupted form) and does not match the provided literal senses.
segments.s159-g155: بدو گفت = 'to him she said' — the indirect object/goal (بدو = 'to him') appears before گفت ('said'); this word order is common in classical Persian. کای is the vocative 'O', followed by شهریار سپاه 'king (ruler) of the host/army' as a title/epithet.
segments.s159-g156: که here introduces the content of what was said ('that' / 'saying that...'). چون = 'like/as', used here in the sense 'such as'. تو = 'you'. ندیدست is an archaic/perfect negative construction meaning 'has not been seen' or 'never was seen' (passive sense: 'never has been seen'). خورشید و ماه = 'the sun and the moon' as parallel subjects; the clause means the sun and moon have never seen one like you.
segments.s159-g157: نه اندر زمین کس چو فرزند تو: the negative particle نه at the start (outside the listed words) negates the clause. اندر زمین = 'in/on the earth' (locative), کس (with the negation) = 'no one'. چو = 'like/as'. فرزند تو = 'your son/offspring'. The word order places the locative and negation early for emphasis.
segments.s159-g158: جهان شاد بادا = 'may the world be happy' where بادا is the optative particle meaning 'may it be'. به پیوند تو = 'on account of / by your bond/lineage' (به indicating cause/for, پیوند = 'bond/lineage', تو = 'your'). The optative + causal phrase expresses a wish that the whole world rejoice because of his offspring.
segments.s159-g159: فرستش is the past action 'sent' (here either with an object clitic incorporated or with the object understood from context). به سوی = 'toward', شبستان = 'bedchamber/inner chamber'. خویش (split as خوی + ش) = 'her own' (خوی = kinship/own, ش = the third-person possessive clitic). The sequence means 'sent (her) toward her own chamber.'
segments.s159-g160: بر + (خواهران و فغستان) خویش = 'to / upon her sisters and her faghestān (place of wailing)'. بر here marks direction/target as well. خواهران = 'sisters', فغستان is a poetic noun meaning a place or state of lamentation/uproar. Again خوی + ش is the possessive 'her own'.
segments.s159-g17: بدید is a past-tense 3rd-person verb 'he saw/experienced'. از introduces the source ('from'). بد و نیک is a paired noun phrase 'bad and good' (joined by و = 'and') and together with آزار او ('his harm/harassment') it means 'the harm from him, in bad and good'. The word order is poetic: 'he saw (بدید) from bad and good the harm of him (از بد و نیک آزار او)'.
segments.s159-g18: پناهید here is a narrative past form meaning 'he sought/took refuge'. به یزدان marks the locus/agent of refuge ('in/with God'), and از کار او ('from his deed/doing') names what he fled from. The preposition از governs کار او; overall: 'he took refuge in God from his action.'
segments.s159-g19: چنین is an adverb 'thus/so'. تا here introduces the temporal clause ('when/once'), and برآمد is the past verb 'arose/arrived'. بر + ین form the demonstrative 'برین' (= 'that/this') modifying روزگار ('time/fate'). The phrase means 'thus, when that time came'.
segments.s159-g20: تهمتن is the proper name/epithet (Tahmātan). بیامد is the past 'came' (poetic narrative). بر + شهریار expresses motion/toward or presence 'before/to the king' (بر indicating direction or 'before' in courtly sense).
segments.s159-g21: چنین گفت 'thus he said'—چنین is 'thus' and گفت is the past verb 'said' (introducing reported speech: کاین ...). کاین is the fused relative/demonstrative 'that/this', کودک 'child', and شیرفش is a compound/adjective 'milk‑suckling' or 'nursed at the breast' describing the child.
segments.s159-g22: مرا + را is the emphasized object 'me' (مرا with the object marker را). پرورانید is the verb 'to rear/nurture' (here in reported sense 'be reared/raise'), and باید is the modal 'must/ought'. به + کش forms a short prepositional/directional phrase; in the poetic register کش (lit. 'to pull') functions idiomatically here as part of the verb phrase meaning roughly 'to take/keep/bring' — so the whole clause reads 'he must be reared by me' with poetic word order.
segments.s159-g23: چو here functions as a causal/temporal 'since/when'. دارندگان is the plural noun 'holders/possessors' (addressing the party), and ترا is the 2nd‑person clitic 'you' attached for vocative/possession (here addressing 'O holders'). مایه نیست is a negated nominal predicate 'there is no means/resource' (مایه = resources/means; نیست = is not/does not exist). The clause means 'since you possessors have no resources'.
segments.s159-g24: مر is an archaic/dialectal dative/emphatic pronoun-form ('to me' / 'for me'); او را is 'for him' or 'him' with object marker; بگیتی is a circumstantial adverb 'in the world' (ب- + گیتی); چو من is a simile 'as I' or 'like me'; دایه 'nurse/wet‑nurse'; نیست 'is not'. Word order is poetic: 'for him in the world there is not a nurse like me' = 'there is no nurse in the world for him like me.'
segments.s159-g273: "که" here introduces an emphatic subordinate clause ('that' / 'for'), and the clause ends with the existential verb "نیست" (is not / does not exist). "کس را" is a negative existential phrase meaning 'no one' with "را" used to topicalize/emphasize the argument; the adjective phrase "بسان تو فرزند" ('a child like you') fills the predicative slot before the final copula "نیست".
segments.s159-g274: The phrase is another existential statement with the copula "نیست" at the end. "همان" ('that very' / 'the same') modifies "شاه"; "شاه را" uses "را" to mark the king as topic/emphasized subject of the negation; "نیز" ('also') signals that this clause parallels the previous one; "پیوند" is the noun ('link/kin/tie') that is said not to exist for that king.
segments.s159-g275: "سیاوش" is the subject and "بدانست" is the past tense verb 'he knew/realized.' The following clause "کان مهر چیست" is an embedded content clause: "کان" is an archaic/deictic particle introducing 'that' (here preceding "مهر" = 'love'), and "چیست" ('what is') is a present copular question-word construction functioning as the complement of "بدانست."
segments.s159-g276: This is a predicative clause: "چنان دوستی" ('such friendship') is the subject phrase and the copula is the suffixed adjective-like predicate "ایزدیست" ('is divine / godly'). "نز" here means 'from' and combines with "ره" ('way, course') to yield 'from the way/path of' — so the whole means the friendship originates in or belongs to a divine order.
segments.s159-g277: "به نزدیک" is a directional prepositional phrase ('toward/near'), followed by the noun "خواهر" ('sister') as the goal. The verb "خرامید" is a past tense poetic verb meaning 'he strode/pranced proudly'; the adverb "زود" ('quickly/soon') follows it to qualify the action. Word order places direction before object and the verb before the adverb in this poetic line.
segments.s159-g278: The conjunction "که" introduces a causal/explanatory clause ('for/because'). "آن جایگه" means 'that place/position'; "کار ناساز" is a compound adjective-noun meaning 'an unsuitable / discordant affair'; final "بود" is the past copula 'was.' Together the clause explains why he acted as he did.
segments.s159-g279: "برو" here functions as a narrative/performative particle (literally 'go' but used to push the action forward — roughly 'then' or 'so'), followed by "خواهران" ('the sisters') as subject. "آفرین" is an exclamation of praise ('bravo, well done') and "خواندند" is the past plural verb 'they uttered/called' (they voiced the exclamation).
segments.s159-g280: "به کرسی زرینش" is a directional/result phrase 'onto his golden seat/throne' — "زرینش" combines the adjective "زرین" ('golden') with the third-person possessive suffix "ش" ('his'). The verb "بنشاندند" is past plural 'they seated (him)/they installed (him),' with the direct object (him) implied rather than explicitly stated.
segments.s159-g113: به هر جای is a prepositional phrase meaning 'in/at every place' (به + هر + جای). جشنی is a noun 'a celebration' that serves as the object/topic of the verb. بیاراستند is a past-tense 3rd-person plural verb ('they adorned/arranged') — the prefix بیـ here is part of the verb form; the verb comes at the end of the clause, as is normal in Persian.
segments.s159-g114: This is a coordination of nouns followed by a past plural verb: می‌و و رود و رامشگران are items joined by و 'and', and خواستند is the past 3rd-person plural 'they requested/wished'. می‌و and رود are nouns here forming a list (wine/fruit and stringed music); رامشگران is a plural agent noun ('those who calm/entertain'), and خواستند governs that whole coordinated list.
segments.s159-g115: یکی سور فرمود combines a determinative/adjectival یکی 'a certain' with سور 'banquet/feast'; فرمود is an honorific/past verb used here in the sense of issuing an order or command ('he ordered/commanded'). کاندر جهان begins the relative/adverbial phrase 'in which / in the world', linking the feast to the following clause.
segments.s159-g116: کسی پیش از وی نکرد is a negative universal construction: کسی here functions as 'no one' (not an affirmative 'someone'), پیش از وی 'before him', and نکرد is the past negative 'did not do'. از مهان ('with respect to/for the guests') specifies the domain of that action. The clause therefore means 'no one before him had done (this) for guests.'
segments.s159-g117: به یک هفته is a temporal phrase 'for a week'. زان گونه = 'in that manner' or 'so'; بودن in the past plural بودند 'they were' takes شاد 'happy/merry' as its predicate. The clause reads 'for one week they were merry in that way.'
segments.s159-g118: به هشتم is a temporal adjunct 'on the eighth (day)'. در گنجها 'in the treasuries/treasure-chests' locates the action, and برگشاد is a past passive/ intransitive perfective 'were opened' (the verb is placed at the end). Together: 'On the eighth day the treasures were opened.'
segments.s159-g119: ز هر چیز گنجی is a partitive/genitive-idiom meaning 'a treasure from every kind of thing' (ز + هر + چیز). بفرمود is an honorific/polite past verb meaning here 'he ordered/commanded (to give)', and شاه appears postverbally as the subject ('the king'). The construction places the object phrase before the verb and the subject after it, a common poetic/orderly word order in Persian.
segments.s159-g120: This is an enumerative list introduced by repeated ز 'of/from': ز مهر و ز تیع و ز تخت و کلاه lists the types of gifts. Each item is coordinated by و 'and' (و appears between items). The repeated ز is a poetic device marking each category as 'from/of ...'.
segments.s159-g9: جهان گشت is a past-tense verbal phrase meaning “the world became.” ازان خوب پر is an adjectival phrase where ازان = “of that/from that” and خوب پر = “full of good/beautiful (things)” (پر after an adjective means “filled with”). گفت و گوی is a poetic pairing of two nouns meaning “speech and discourse/conversation.” The whole segment therefore says the world was made/full of such good talk.
segments.s159-g10: کزان گونه = “of that kind / from that sort” (کزان = کِ + آن, a classical phrasing). نشنید کس literally inverts the usual order (کس نشنید) and means “no one had heard / no one had known of.” موی و روی (“hair and face”) names the features of appearance; together the clause says nobody had heard/seen such hair and face before. Note the classical inversion and elliptical diction typical of Ferdowsi.
segments.s159-g11: جهاندار is a title ‘lord of the world/sovereign.’ نامش = نام + ش (enclitic = “his”), so نامش سیاوخش کرد is a simple past clause: ‘he made his name Siyâvakhsh / he named him Siyâvakhsh.’ کرد is the past of کردن used to form ‘to name/declare.’
segments.s159-g12: چرخ گردنده را بخش کرد is another past clause: چرخ گردنده = ‘the rotating/turning sky/heavens’ (گردنده = ‘rotating’), را marks the definite object, and بخش کرد = ‘gave a portion / granted as a gift.’ برو here is a brief connective/adverbial particle used in classical poetry (literally ‘thereupon / to him’ in sense of moving the action forward). The sense is: he granted him a share/portion of the revolving heaven.
segments.s159-g13: ازان کاو is a classical construction meaning ‘from him who / from that one who’ (کاو functions like an archaic relative 'که او' here). شمارد is present/past-poetic ‘he counted / he reckoned’ (aorist/present form used narratively), and سپهر بلند = ‘the lofty sky/heaven.’ The clause conveys that by reckoning/observing the high heavens he performed a calculation/assessment.
segments.s159-g14: بدانست is a past-tense verb meaning ‘he knew it / he ascertained.’ The following list نیک و بد و چون و چند strings together pairs (good and bad; how and how many) — نیک ‘good’, بد ‘bad’, چون ‘how / in what manner’, چند ‘how many’ — giving the objects of his knowledge. The coordinating و simply joins the items.
segments.s159-g15: ستاره بران is a poetic locative/adverbial phrase meaning ‘by/at those stars’ or ‘from the stars’ (بران = ‘thereon / upon that’); بچه آشفته دید is a past-tense clause ‘(they) saw the disturbed/tormented child.’ The segment uses compact word order typical of Ferdowsi: modifier phrases precede the verb.
segments.s159-g16: غمی گشت is ‘sorrow arose / he became sorrowful’ (گشت = became). چون here functions temporally/causally (‘when / since’) introducing the subordinate clause بخت او خفته دید ‘he saw that his fortune was asleep’ (خفته = ‘asleep’). دید at the end is the reporting verb ‘saw,’ so the whole line: grief came when he perceived his fate lying dormant.
segments.s159-g145: A compact past‑tense clause: فرستاد is the simple past 'sent' and کسی را is its definite direct object (را marks 'someone' as the thing sent). نزدیک اوی places the sent person 'near him' — اوی is an older/poetic form of the third‑person pronoun 'him' and follows the locative adjective نزدیک, so the whole means 'sent someone up close to him.'
segments.s159-g146: A subordinate infinitival/nominal clause introduced by که giving the content of what to do. پنهان is an adjective/participle 'hidden' and here governs the following object phrase سیاووش را (Siyavash as definite object). این بگوی is a jussive/subjunctive instruction 'say this' or 'tell this' (i.e. order the messenger to say the following); word order is object (سیاووش را) before the complement (این) and the verbal form بگوی expresses the commanded speech.
segments.s159-g147: This opens a subordinate clause with که used in the sense 'so that' or 'that.' اندر is the literary preposition 'in/within' and شبستان شاه جهان is a genitive chain 'the bedchamber of the king of the world' (شاه = king, جهان as an epithet 'of the world' — a poetic title). Word order is the expected prepositional phrase 'in the bedchamber of the king'.
segments.s159-g148: This is an impersonal prognostic construction: نباشد + شگفت = 'it would not be a wonder' (نباشد is a negative form of باشد used impersonally here). ار introduces the conditional 'if' and شوی is the second‑person singular subjunctive/prejunctive of شدن 'to become/appear' — 'if you were to become/appear' — followed by the adverb ناگهان 'suddenly.' Overall: 'it would not be wondrous if you suddenly appeared.'
segments.s159-g149: A coordinate clause: فرستاده (the messenger) is the subject, رفت is the simple past 'went/left', and و links the next action. بدادش پیام is a compact phrase meaning 'gave/delivered him the message' (بدادش here functions as a light past verbal sequence 'gave to him' + object پیام). Word order places the verb and its object together after the subject.
segments.s159-g150: This is a cause/result statement: برآشفت is a past form 'became agitated/angry' (third person), زان = 'from that/from it' gives the cause, and کار او نیکنام is the noun phrase 'his deed/affair, (he being) famed' — کار 'deed/act' with the pronoun او and the epithet نیکنام 'renowned' describing the person whose action caused the agitation.
segments.s159-g151: A reporting frame: بدو 'to him' (archaic/dialectal form of 'to him'), گفت is the verb 'said' (here used as a reporting verb), and مرد شبستان is an appositive/compound noun 'the man of the bedchamber.' نیم at the end of this segment is a terse, archaic/poetic element (here printed as نیم) — its exact syntactic force is elliptical/poetic (see next segment where the negative/incomplete state is explained).
segments.s159-g152: This is the quoted injunction: مجویم is a negative jussive 'do not seek/look for' (a polite/old form of prohibition), که introduces the explanatory clause, and what follows gives reasons or conditions. بابند is a descriptive predicate (here lexical meaning is contextually 'are fastened/closed' — the lemma list gives 'fastener/strap' but it functions as 'are shut/bolted' in context). و connects the second reason دستان نیم, where دستان 'hands' (literally 'hands' or figuratively 'helpers') plus نیم 'half/partial' yields an idiomatic sense 'hands are not fully present/are insufficient' — overall: 'do not search, for the doors are shut and the hands are but half (i.e. cannot help).'
segments.s159-g209: This clause begins with مدار, an archaic/poetic imperative meaning 'do not let' or 'do not admit' (negative command) followed by ایچ (archaic هیچ = 'any/none') modifying اندیشهِ بد ('evil thought'). The directional phrase به دل ('into the heart') follows the object. Word order is imperative + object + directional complement; ایچ is a negator limiting اندیشه.
segments.s159-g210: همه ('entire, wholly') modifies the implicit target (the heart). آرای here is a jussive/imperative form (poetic) meaning 'adorn' (from آراستن) taking شادی ('with joy') as its complement; the conjunction و links the second imperative غم برگسل, where برگسل is a verbal compound (بر + گسل) used poetically as 'tear away/remove' or 'cast off' sorrow. Thus the two coordinated imperatives instruct to fill with joy and remove sorrow.
segments.s159-g211: ببین is an imperative 'look/see'. پردگی is a poetic noun 'veil/curtain' and کودکان را is the marked object ('the children' with را). یکی here refers back to one individual/item ('one of them' or 'one'), placed after the object for emphasis. The clause is an exhortation to notice a single child/veil among the children.
segments.s159-g212: مگر here introduces a possibility ('perhaps' in poetic usage) followed by شادمانه (adverb 'joyfully') and شوند, the subjunctive/optative 3rd-person plural of شدن ('may become' / 'become'), and اندکی ('a little'). Together they express a cautious hope: 'perhaps they/one of them will become a little joyful.'
segments.s159-g213: پس ('then') links back to the preceding thought. پرده is the subject 'veil/curtain.' اندر ترا is a prepositional phrase 'within you / inside you' where ت is the enclitic 2nd-person element, and را is the object/topic marker emphasizing پرده. خواهرست is a copular predicate ('is a sister') with the archaic -ست copula; overall: 'then the veil within you is a sister.'
segments.s159-g214: پر از مهر is an adjectival phrase 'full of affection' (پر = 'full (of)'). The conjunction و links the following proper name سودابه; چون introduces a simile 'like/as,' and مادرست ('is motherly' / 'is like a mother') uses the same archaic copula -ست. Grammatically this segment either continues description of the veil ('it is full of affection') and then states 'and Sudabeh is like a mother,' or reads as coordinated predicates qualifying the scene.
segments.s159-g215: This is a reporting clause: سیاوش (proper name) + چنین ('thus') + گفت (past of گفتن 'said' — poetic reporting verb). کز is a classical contracted form of که از meaning 'from/starting at' and modifies بامداد ('dawn/morning'): 'that/from dawn.' Note some listed senses for گفت are nominal, but here it functions as the past-tense verb 'said.'
segments.s159-g216: بیایم is a (subjunctive/optative or future-oriented) first-person form 'I will come' or 'I shall come.' کنَم (کنم) is the present subjunctive/intentional 'I will do' and begins the purpose clause: هر چه او کرد ('whatever he did/commanded') with یاد ('remembrance' / 'as to the matter' — here functioning as the object of کنَم in the sense 'to do/perform what he did/ordered'). The sequence expresses intent: 'I will come at dawn and do whatever he did/ordered.'
segments.s159-g281: This is a prepositional phrase introduced by بر (here 'upon'/'fell upon') describing what happened to the sisters: بر + NP. بد زمانی دراز = 'a long bad/ill time' where بد qualifies زمانی (time) and دراز follows as an adjective meaning 'long' (noun + adjective order typical in Persian poetry). The whole phrase functions as a circumstantial clause ('upon the sisters there came a long bad time').
segments.s159-g282: Adverbial manner word خرامان ('with a stately/proud gait') precedes the verb بیامد (simple past 'came'), forming 'he came, striding'. سوی تخت باز is a directional phrase: سوی = 'toward', تخت = 'throne', and باز = 'again/back' (here 'back toward the throne'). Word order (manner → verb → direction) is typical in older/poetic Persian.
segments.s159-g283: شبستان ('inner/chamber') is the subject. همه شد پر از + noun is the construction 'became wholly/full of ...': شد = 'became' (past), پر از = 'full of', گفت‌وگوی = 'conversation'. همه here emphasizes totality ('entirely/wholly became full').
segments.s159-g284: که introduces an explanatory/clausal remark ('for/that'). اینت is a poetic/contracted form (≈ 'این است' = 'this is') linking to the predicate سر و تاج فرهنگ جوی = 'the head and crown of (a) culture-seeker' (سر و تاج = 'head and crown' = highest honour; فرهنگ جوی = 'one who seeks culture/learning', juy here functioning as 'seeker'). Word order is predicate after the copular-like اینت.
segments.s159-g285: تو گویی is an idiomatic, archaic expression meaning 'it seems / one might say' (not literally 'you say' here). The remainder به مردم نماند همی = 'there remained nothing to the people' (نماند = 'did not remain'; همی adds a habitual/emphatic tone). Word order places the indirect object phrase به مردم after the idiom.
segments.s159-g286: روانش ('his spirit/soul', with possessive -ش) is the subject; خرد ('wisdom') is the object; برفشاند is a poetic past/presentive verb meaning 'scattered/dispensed (upon)' and همی again gives an iterative or emphatic sense ('kept on / continually'). The structure is S + O + V, common in Persian poetry.
segments.s159-g287: Syntactically: subject (سیاوش) + locative/goal phrase به پیش پدر ('to/in the presence of his father') + شد (here an auxiliary meaning 'went/approached') + بگفت (past 'said'). The compound 'به پیشِ پدر شد' is an idiomatic way to say 'he went up to / came before his father'.
segments.s159-g288: که introduces reported speech ('that'). دیدم is first-person past 'I saw' (reporting what he says). به + پرده‌سرای marks the location ('in/into the curtain-chamber'); نهفت is an adjectival/past participle 'hidden' describing what was seen. The clause is a direct report of sight embedded after بگفت.
segments.s159-g233: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing the clause. پردخته شد is a passive/perfect-like sequence: پردخته (past participle) + شد (auxiliary 'was') = 'was drawn/was made ready'. هیربد follows the verb as the logical object/subject in classical word order; را is the direct-object marker attached to هیربد, and بخواند is a third-person singular verb at the end meaning 'he called/summoned' (in this context). The whole segment therefore reads 'When X was drawn/ready, he called the hirbod.'
segments.s159-g234: سخنهای is the plural noun 'words/speeches' (‑های plural). The adjective شایسته follows the noun in classical Persian order and qualifies it as 'fitting/appropriate.' چندی is an adverb 'a few/several' modifying the verb, and براند is the sentence-final verb in third‑person singular; here it functions to mean 'he uttered/put forth' (verb at the end is normal in older Persian).
segments.s159-g235: سیاووش را گفت uses را to mark Siavash as the addressee/indirect object of 'said' (classical phrasing for 'he told/said to Siavash'). After that comes the quoted/imperative instruction با او برو — با او 'with him' (prepositional phrase) and برو is the imperative 'go'. Word order places the addressed person before the reported imperative.
segments.s159-g236: بیارای here is an imperative verb meaning 'make ready/prepare' (classical spelling); دل را is the object marked by را ('your heart' as the thing to be prepared). به introduces the purpose phrase دیدار نو 'for the new meeting' (نو 'new' follows the noun). The sequence is an imperative clause following the reported speech.
segments.s159-g237: برفتند is the past‑tense plural 'they went/departed.' هر دو 'both' specifies the subject, and به یک جا 'to one place' gives the destination. The final به هم ('together') reinforces that they went as a pair; such placing of adjuncts after the verb is common in epic/classical Persian.
segments.s159-g238: This is a paired predicate describing their state: روان شادمان 'with spirits joyful' (روان 'spirit/soul' qualified by the adjective شادمان). و links the second phrase تهی دل ز غم, where تهی modifies دل ('heart emptied/devoid') and ز غم gives the cause/what it is emptied of ('of sorrow'). The construction uses noun + adjective patterns typical of Persian poetic style.
segments.s159-g239: چو is the temporal 'when.' برداشت here is a past action 'he lifted/took up' (برداشتن), پرده 'the curtain' is the direct object, and ز در 'from the door' indicates origin/place — the preposition ز precedes در in poetic order. هیربد after the phrase is the subject (common in classical Persian to place the subject after object/adjunct).
segments.s159-g240: سیاوش is the topic/subject. همی بود is the imperfective past construction ('was still/was in the state of'), with همی marking ongoing or habitual aspect in older Persian. ترسان is a predicate adjective/participial adjective meaning 'afraid,' and ز بد gives the reason 'because of ill/misfortune.' The copula بود links the subject to the predicate.
segments.s159-g129: چنین 'thus' is a fronting adverb giving manner or result. هفت سالش is a time phrase: هفت سال 'seven years' with the possessive suffix ش 'his', so literally 'his seven years'. همی + آزمود forms a poetic/historical past with a durative/continuous sense: همی marks ongoing or habitual action and آزمود is the simple past 'tried/tested'. The clause therefore reads 'thus they/one tested him for seven years' (subject is omitted and inferred from context).
segments.s159-g130: به هر کار 'به' here introduces the domain/respect ('in/regarding') and هر کار means 'every task/deed'. جز 'except' negates alternatives, and پاک زاده is a compound epithet 'pure-born' or 'noble-born' (پاک modifies زاده). نبود is the past negative 'there was not' / 'was not'. The whole clause literally: 'in every deed there was none except the pure‑born.'
segments.s159-g131: بفرمود is the honorific/polite past of گفتن/فرمودن meaning 'he ordered/declared'. The preceding token بهشتم is a prepositional/locative-like form (here marking the addressee/place/context of the command) and is attached before the verb for emphasis. تا introduces a subordinate purpose/result clause ('that' / 'so that') and is followed by the object تاج زر 'a gold crown'. The clause is therefore an order: 'he commanded that (one) give a golden crown'.
segments.s159-g132: ز is the poetic genitive/prepositional 'of/from' linking گوهر ('gem') to درافشان (a participial/epithet 'scattering, glittering with'). The sequence گوهر درافشان qualifies the following nouns کلاه و کمر 'cap and belt' — i.e. 'a cap and belt glittering with jewels'. Word order places the ornament description before the nouns it qualifies, a common poetic pattern.
segments.s159-g133: نبشتند is the past plural 'they wrote/inscribed'. منشور is the object 'charter/inscribed document'. بر is the preposition 'on/upon' and پرنیان is the material 'silken (cloth) / silken robe' — together 'they inscribed the charter on silk.' Note the verb-subject order: object and locative come before the verb for poetic word order.
segments.s159-g134: به رسم literally 'by/according to the custom' — به takes a 'by'/'in accordance with' sense here. بزرگان 'the great ones/elders' and فر کیان 'the royal glory/majesty of the kings' form the authorities whose custom is being followed. The phrase qualifies the manner of inscribing: 'in the manner/custom of the elders and the kings' splendor.'
