segments.s159-g135: زمین کهستان is a fronted noun phrase meaning 'the land of the mountainous country' (زمین = land/country, کهستان = mountain-region). ورا is the unstressed object pronoun 'to him', and داد is the past verb 'gave'. شاه 'the king' appears at the end as the grammatical subject — a common poetic inversion that fronted the object/place for emphasis: 'The king gave him the mountain land.'
segments.s159-g136: که introduces an explanatory subordinate clause 'for/because' or a relative 'who/that'. بود او is the simple past 'he was'. سزای here is used as a noun 'desert/recompense' (i.e. 'worthy of'), followed by بزرگی 'greatness' and و گاه which pairs as a second quality ('and honour/occasion/place' poetically meaning high station or fitting occasion). Together the clause explains the reason: 'for he was worthy of great reward and honour.'
segments.s159-g249: This phrase strings together nouns in a poetic, paratactic way: می‌و (mi-ū) + رود (rūd) + و + آوای رامشگران. In classical Persian poetry the conjunction و can link nouns without an explicit verb; here رود is read as the noun 'river/torrent' (not a present-tense verb), and آوای رامشگران ‘the melody/voice of the soothing singers’ follows as another coordinated element.
segments.s159-g250: همه بر سران افسران گران is a locational/covering phrase: همه 'all' (referring back to the preceding things) + بر 'upon' + سران 'heads/tops' + افسران 'helms/diadems' + گران 'costly/heavy'. Word order is typical Persian (modifier after head in the nominal phrase: افسران گران = 'heavy/precious helms'), and بر governs the following noun phrase to give the sense 'over/upon the tops of the heavy helms'.
segments.s159-g251: شبستان بهشتی شد آراسته is built as noun + adjective + 'شد' (become) + predicate adjective: شبستان 'the inner chamber' + بهشتی 'heavenly/like heaven' modifies it; شد is the past-tense copula/lexical verb 'became', and آراسته is a predicative adjective meaning 'adorned'. The order (adjective before شد, adjective after شد) is normal in classical Persian for a resultant state.
segments.s159-g252: پر از خوبرویان و پرخواسته is a repetitive 'full of' construction: پر 'full (of)' + از 'of' introduces the contents (خوبرویان 'the beautiful-faced ones'), and the coordinate phrase و پرخواسته repeats the idea with پرخواسته 'much‑desired' functioning adjectivally/nominatively. Together: 'filled with beautiful ones and the sought‑after.'
segments.s159-g253: سیاوش چو نزدیک ایوان رسید: چو is the classical subordinating conjunction 'when'; نزدیک ایوان is an adverbial phrase 'near the portico' (نزدیک = near, modifying رسید), and رسید is the simple past 'reached/arrived'. The clause therefore means 'when Siavash came near the portico'.
segments.s159-g254: یکی تخت زرین درفشنده دید follows the SOV pattern where the (indefinite) object(s) precede the verb دید 'saw'. یکی = 'one/a certain' introduces the noun phrase; تخت زرین = 'golden throne' (noun + adjective) and درفشنده 'standard‑bearer' is placed in the same noun phrase (appositive or coordinate), so the sense is 'he saw a golden throne and a standard‑bearer.'
segments.s159-g255: برو is an imperative 'go' (here functioning as a verbal command to servants). بر is the prepositional/verb‑linking element that connects the imperative to the following object phrase; ز (poetic for 'of/from') introduces the material/ornament: ز پیروزه کرده نگار = 'the beloved made/attired with turquoise' (پیروزه 'turquoise', کرده past participle 'made/treated', نگار 'beloved'). The whole segment is an imperative clause ordering that the beloved, dressed in turquoise, be brought or set forward.
segments.s159-g256: به دیبا بیاراسته شاهوار continues the description: به + noun often marks the manner or clothing ('in/with brocade' here), دیبا = 'brocade', بیاراسته functions as a participial adjective meaning 'arrayed/adorned' (classical poetic usage treats this as a descriptive participle), and شاهوار 'regal/queen‑like' further qualifies the appearance. Together: 'arrayed in brocade, regal.'
segments.s159-g225: به + proper name + فرمود: the preposition به marks the beneficiary/indirect object ('to/for'); سودابه is the addressee (proper name). فرمود is past tense (he said/ordered). تا introduces a subordinate purpose clause here ('so that / in order that'). پیش + اوی indicates the goal/place ('before/in the presence of him'); اوی is an older/poetic form of او (him/her).
segments.s159-g226: نثار آورد is a noun+verb sequence meaning 'bring offerings' (نثار = offering, آورد = bring). The list of items follows as direct objects: گوهر (jewel), then and (و) مشک (musk), and (و) بوی (scent/fragrance). Word order is verb after noun phrase (poetic), with conjunctions linking the items.
segments.s159-g227: پرستندگان (worshippers/devotees) is the plural subject. نیز ('also/likewise') adds that they, too, acted. با + خواهران ('with the sisters') marks accompaniment; خواهران is plural. Word order places the adjuncts before the verb (typical in Persian poetry).
segments.s159-g228: زبرجد (peridot/green gem) is the thing being scattered; فشانند is present-tense plural (they scatter / they shower), agreeing with پرستندگان. بر + زعفران ('upon/on the saffron') gives the target/surface. The verb-final placement and plural verb reflect subject-verb agreement in Persian.
segments.s159-g229: چو is a temporal conjunction here ('when'). خورشید is the grammatical subject. برزد (archaic poetic verb) means to appear/come up (here 'lift/raise'), used with سر ('head') to form the image 'the sun lifted its head'. از + کوهسار ('from/out of the mountain ridge') indicates origin/place. The phrasing is poetic and slightly archaic.
segments.s159-g230: سیاوش is the subject/name. برآمد is a past tense verb meaning 'came/approached/rose up' (here 'approached'). بر + شهریار means 'to/toward the king' (بر as 'toward/to' with شهریار 'king'). The sequence places the action after the temporal clause.
segments.s159-g231: برو here functions as an exclamatory/poetic particle (an archaic flavor that accompanies آفرین), followed by آفرین کرد 'he praised' (کرد is the past tense 'did/made' used with آفرین). و links the clauses. بردش is برد + the enclitic object -ش ('him'), here meaning 'took/carried/led him' (verb sense 'to take' is implied in context). نماز (prayer) is the object — 'led him to prayer' or 'brought him to perform prayer.'
segments.s159-g232: سخن گفت is a light-verb construction meaning 'spoke' or 'held speech' (سخن = speech, گفت = said/spoke). با او marks the interlocutor ('with him'). سپهد (spahbed) is the agent/title (the commander); word order here is topical/poetic, so the title follows the phrase. به راز ('in secret') is an adverbial phrase marking the manner (private/confidential).
segments.s159-g297: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. شب گشت پیدا is a small clause: گشت (become) + پیدا (apparent/visible) describes the coming of night — literally 'night became visible'. و شد روز تار links with و (and) + شد (became) to state that the day in turn 'grew/wascome تار' (poetic 'dark/gloomy'). Word order is classical Persian: temporal 'چو' + subject phrase + verb.
segments.s159-g298: شد here functions as a simple past copula/verb 'became/was' and introduces the state/place that follows. اندر is the poetic preposition 'in/within'. شبستان means 'inner chamber/bedchamber'. شه نامدار is a noun phrase 'the famed king' (شه = king, نامدار = renowned) and functions as the subject/possessor of the chamber; overall: 'the famed king was (or withdrew) into the bedchamber.'
segments.s159-g299: پژوهنده is a participial adjective 'questioning/inquiring' modifying the name سودابه; را is the object marker that marks سودابه as the addressee/affected person. شاه is the agent 'the king' and گفت is the verb 'said'. Word order (adj + name + را + subject + verb) is literary but functions as 'the king said to the inquiring Sudabeh.'
segments.s159-g300: که introduces reported speech ('that/that is'), this starts the content of what was said. این is the demonstrative 'this', رازت is 'your secret' (راز + possessive suffix -ت). از من = 'from me' (از = from/of, من = me). نباید is the impersonal modal 'must not/should not' and is followed by نهفت, the (classical) infinitive/verb 'to hide' (here functioning as the action forbidden). The whole clause: 'that you must not hide this secret from me.'
segments.s159-g301: ز is the poetic preposition 'of/from' introducing the topic. فرهنگ و رای سیاوش is a coordinate noun phrase 'Siyavash's character (manners) and counsel/opinion'. بگوی is an imperative/subjunctive form 'tell/speak of' (addressing the addressee). The verb stands at the end as an admonition/command typical of classical Persian.
segments.s159-g302: Here ز again marks genitive/topic: 'of'. بالا means 'height/highness' (here: noble bearing or rank). و links 'بالا' to 'دیدار و گفتار' — 'meetings and speech'. اوی is an old/poetic possessive/pronominal form 'his' (literally 'of him'), so the phrase lists: 'of his highness, (of) his meetings and (of) his words.'
segments.s159-g303: پسند تو آمد is an idiomatic expression: پسند (liking/approval) + تو (your) + آمد ('came') = 'your liking came' → 'it pleases you/you approve'. خردمند هست is a simple predicative clause 'he is wise' (خردمند = wise, هست = is). The two parts are linked as comment and judgment: 'Your approval is come; he is wise.'
segments.s159-g304: This is a comparative/clarifying clause: از ... ز ... frames the two standards being compared ('by report' vs. 'by seeing'). آواز here means 'report/reputation' (poetic usage), and دیدن is 'seeing/experience'. به‌گر is an archaic comparative/qualifier meaning roughly 'better/more so' (used poetically to compare the two modes), and بهست ('it suffices' / 'is good') closes the clause: 'if by reputation he is praiseworthy, by seeing he is even more so.' The prepositional particles are poetic and invertible, so both ز/از mark the two elements compared.
segments.s159-g185: بپیچید is a past/preterite 3rd‑person singular of پیچیدن (here 'turn aside, go away'); the prefix ب‑ is an older/poetic form but the meaning is simply 'he turned/withdrew'. The clause بر خویشتن راز کرد is literal 'made/kept a secret upon himself' — بر + خویشتن = 'to himself', راز = 'secret', and کرد is the past verb 'did/made' used idiomatically for 'kept (secret)'. Word order is Subject Verb (implicit 'he') then a prepositional object phrase and the verb کرد finishing the clause.
segments.s159-g186: This is a light‑verb construction: آغاز کردن = 'to begin'. از introduces the domain/source ('with respect to' or 'for the purpose of') and انجام here means 'the completion/result' or 'the carrying‑out' which together with آهنگ ('intention, aim') describes what he begins. کرد is again the past 'did/made' completing the verbal phrase 'began'. The phrase order is somewhat poetic but functions as 'he began (کرد) a plan/aim (آهنگ) concerning the accomplishment (از انجام)'.
segments.s159-g187: که‌گر is an archaic conditional particle meaning 'if'. من شوم uses the present/subjunctive form شوم ('may I be' / 'were I to be') to mark a hypothetical: 'if I were/become'. در شبستان is a prepositional phrase 'in the bedchamber' and اوی is an archaic possessive/pronoun meaning 'his/her' attached after the noun, so the whole phrase reads 'if I were in his bedchamber'.
segments.s159-g188: ز is the poetic form of 'from'. یابم is a present‑subjunctive/optative form 'I might find/obtain' (here 'I would get' or 'I would have'). سودابه is the proper name. بسی گفت و گوی is a noun phrase meaning 'much conversation' or 'many conversations' (بسی = 'much/many' in context, گفت و گوی = 'talk and speaking' → 'conversation'). The clause expresses a hypothetical benefit: 'I would obtain from Sudabeh much talk.'
segments.s159-g189: This clause begins with the subject سیاوش. چنین ('thus/so') modifies the manner of the reply. داد is the past verb 'gave' (here 'gave [an] answer'), and پاسخ is the object 'answer/reply'. که introduces reported content ('that...'). Word order is Subject + adverb + Verb + Object, then the complementizer که.
segments.s159-g190: مرا داد is 'gave me' but in the tokenization here 'م' + 'را' are split: م (surface oddity) + را (definite/object marker). Grammatically the sequence is object 'me' + را then داد 'gave'. فرمان و تخت و کلاه are the direct objects listed in coordination: 'a command/decree, a throne, and a crown (literally 'hat' but poetic for crown)'. The verb داد governs these objects.
segments.s159-g191: کز is an archaic/prepositional form meaning 'from' or 'from which'. آنجایگه is 'that place/where' (poetic form of 'آنجا+گه'), and کآفتاب is a contracted poetic form of 'که آفتاب' or simply 'the sun' with a prefixed کـ linking the relative clause. بلند modifies آفتاب as 'high' or 'lofty' — 'where the sun is high' (i.e. where it rises high).
segments.s159-g192: برآید is a present/subjunctive or simple present form of برآمدن 'to arise/appear' ('it rises/when it rises'). کند is a causative/light-verb here 'makes' (literally 'does/makes'), so کند + [object] + adjective yields 'makes the earth ...'. خاک را is the object with the postposed object marker را, and ارجمند is the predicative adjective 'esteemed/precious/noble'. The classical word order places the verb forms before the object and adjective, a common inversion in poetry: 'it arises and makes the earth noble.'
segments.s159-g121: This is an illustrative 'from ... to ...' pairing (از ... به ...) common in Persian lists: از اسپان تازی به زین پلنگ = 'from Arabian horses to a panther/ leopard saddle.' اسپان (subject) is modified by the adjective تازی (Arab/Arabian) placed after it, and به introduces the upper bound of the range (زین پلنگ). Note the poetic ordering: modifier after noun (اسپان تازی) and the noun زین 'saddle' precedes its epithet پلنگ 'leopard'.
segments.s159-g122: This short clause continues the catalogue with repeated preposition ز 'from'. The coordination (ز ... و ز ...) links two items: برگستوان and خفتان جنگ. Each noun stands alone after the preposition; جنگ at the end names the domain ('war'). Because this is poetic diction, the exact lexical forms may be archaic or condensed, but grammatically it's a simple coordinated list (ز X و ز Y).
segments.s159-g123: Another paired list starting with the preposition ز 'from' and then using و از 'and from' to add the next item: ز دینار و از بدره‌های درم = 'from dinars and from the (moon-like) dirhams.' The structure is coordinate: noun (دینار) then a second prepositional phrase (از + noun phrase). The poetic modifier بدره‌های qualifies درم as a descriptive plural phrase.
segments.s159-g124: This continues the enumeration with parallel prepositional items: ز دیبای و از گوهر بیش و کم = 'from brocade and from gems, more and less.' Note the paired antonyms بیش و کم 'more and less' follow گوهر and are attached as a final summary (i.e., of gems there were both great and small). The coordination is standard: ز/از + noun (+ qualifiers).
segments.s159-g125: جز introduces an exception: 'except (the) افسر (helmet).' The clause که هنگام افسر نبود is a subordinate clause introduced by که giving the reason/explanation: هنگام افسر 'the time/season for a helmet' + نبود (past tense 'was not'). In other words: 'except the helmet, because it was not yet the time for a helmet.' The verb نبود is past-tense existential/copula.
segments.s159-g126: بدان here functions as a prepositional/ demonstrative compound meaning 'to/for that' (poetic/archaic be‑ān); it links the comment to the preceding clause. کودکی is the indirect object 'that child', تاج is 'crown', در خور is an idiomatic adjectival phrase meaning 'fit/worthy of' (here treated as a unit), and نبود is the past copula 'was not.' Together: 'for that child a crown was not fitting.' Note that خور here is part of the fixed expression در خور and is idiomatic/archaic.
segments.s159-g127: سیاووش را داد uses the object marker را to mark the recipient (Siyâvash) and داد (here a transitive verb 'gave'). The coordination و then pairs the next verb phrase کردش نوید: کردش (کرد + object pronoun) 'made him' and نوید 'good news/joy' or 'a promise' — so 'he gave it to Siyâvash and made him glad/hopeful.' The sequence is simple past narrative.
segments.s159-g128: This final clause states cause and result: ز خوبی 'because/of (his) goodness' + بدادش (here a verbal compound meaning 'bestowed/gave to him' in context) + فراوان 'plentifully' + امید 'hope.' Grammatically, ز introduces the source/reason, the verb grants the object, and فراوان qualifies the extent (adverbial). Note: the lexeme listed as بدادش in the word list lacks the matching senses for 'gave' and is treated here as a giving verb in context.
segments.s159-g305: بدو گفت سودابه همتای شاه is a compact reported-speech phrase. بدو (to him) is an indirect-object prepositional phrase placed before the reporting verb گفت (said). سودابه is the speaker's name; همتای شاه is a noun phrase 'a peer / match of the king' with همتا (peer/match) followed by the possessor شاه (king). Word order is subject/complement after the reporting clause because Persian permits the indirect object before گفت.
segments.s159-g306: ندیدست بر گاه خورشید و ماه is a predicate clause in poetic register: ندیدست (has not been seen) is a perfective/passive-style compound meaning 'has not been seen' (negated form). بر گاه = 'at/on the time of' (بر + گاه 'time/occasion'), so بر گاه خورشید و ماه means 'in the age/time of the sun and moon' (a poetic way to mean 'since time began' or 'in living memory'). و links خورشید (sun) and ماه (moon).
segments.s159-g307: چو فرزند تو کیست اندر جهان: چو here functions as 'like/as' introducing a comparative sense ('who is like your child'); فرزند تو is the possessed noun phrase 'your child'; کیست is the copular question 'who is' (کی + ست, where ست = 'is'); اندر جهان is an adverbial 'in the world' (in/on the earth). The word order places the copula after the interrogative, typical in Persian poetic style.
segments.s159-g308: چرا گفت باید سخن در نهان: چرا = 'why'; گفت here is the past reporting verb 'he said' introducing what he asked; باید is the impersonal modal 'must/ought to'; سخن is 'the matter/word'; در نهان literally 'in hiddenness' = 'in private/secretly'. Grammatically this is a reported question: 'Why, he said, must the matter be hidden?'
segments.s159-g309: بدو گفت شاه ار به مردی رسد: بدو گفت شاه = 'the king said to him' (بدو 'to him' precedes گفت 'said' and شاه is the speaker). ار is the conditional 'if'; به مردی is an indefinite beneficiary/affected person 'to/for any man'; رسد is used in the intransitive sense 'befall/happen' (it reaches/comes to). The clause sets a protasis ('if it happens to any man').
segments.s159-g310: نباید که بیند ورا چشم بد: نباید is impersonal 'it must not / one should not'; که introduces the subordinate clause; بیند is subjunctive/present of دیدن 'to see' (here with modal نباید = 'should not be seen'); ورا = 'him/to him' (object pronoun attached as separate word); چشم بد is the idiom 'evil eye' (چشم 'eye' + بد 'bad/evil'). The clause literally: 'it should not be that an evil eye sees him.'
segments.s159-g311: بدو گفت سودابه‌گر گفت من: بدو گفت = 'she said to him' as before. سودابه‌گر is a poetic/clitic construction where -گر attaches to سودابه to introduce a conditional/subordinate clause ('if Soudabeh [says]'); the following گفت من is the embedded speech 'I say...' (گفت here functions as 'say/declare' inside the conditional). In short: 'she said to him: if I say (so)...'.
segments.s159-g312: پذیره شود رای را جفت من: پذیره (acceptance/reception) is the object; شود is the subjunctive/passive auxiliary 'may/become/be received'; رای را marks 'the counsel/opinion' as a definite object (رای = counsel/advice, را = object marker); جفت من 'my pair/mate' is a possessive noun phrase. The sequence means roughly 'let my counsel be accepted and my match be granted' with poetic ellipsis (an imperative/wish expressed by شود).
segments.s159-g201: چه آموزم is an interrogative clause: چه 'what' introduces the question and آموزم is a 1st-person present/optative form meaning 'shall I teach' (from آموزدن). اندر is the locative preposition 'in', and شبستان شاه is a possessive sequence 'the king's bedchamber' (noun followed by possessor, typical in classical Persian).
segments.s159-g202: بدانش here functions as 'about/with that' (classical blend of به + آن/این); زنان 'women' is the following noun, کی is a relative/pronominal 'who', نمایند is 3rd-person-plural present/subjunctive 'they show', and راه is 'way' or 'method'. The clause means 'women who know it (or by that) will show the way.'
segments.s159-g203: گر is the conditional 'if'. ایدونک is an archaic/emphatic particle ('indeed/so' or 'as such') that qualifies the conditional. فرمان شاه این بود is a normal nominal clause with فرمان شاه 'the king's command' and این بود 'was this' (بود = past copula).
segments.s159-g204: ورا functions as 'for him'/'to him' (marking the person affected). پیش من literally 'before me' or 'in my presence'. رفتن is the infinitive 'to go' used nominally, and آیین بود is a fixed expression 'it was the custom' (آیین = custom/practice, بود = was). The whole phrase = 'it was customary for him to go before me.'
segments.s159-g205: بدو is the prepositional allative 'to him/unto him' placed before the verb for emphasis. گفت is the verb 'said' (classical past), شاه is the subject 'the king', and ای پسر is a vocative address 'O son'. شاد باش is an imperative 'be joyful' (شاد = happy, باش = be!).
segments.s159-g206: همیشه is an adverb 'always'. خرد را uses را to mark خرد 'wisdom' as the definite/emphasized object/topic. تو is the 2nd-person pronoun 'you' (emphatic subject), and بنیاد باش is an imperative phrase 'be (a) foundation' — literally 'be the foundation' (بنیاد = foundation, باش = be!).
segments.s159-g207: سخن کم شنیدم is an idiomatic clause: سخن 'speech/words', کم here functions adverbially 'seldom/rarely', and شنیدم is the 1st-person perfect 'I have heard'. بدین نیکوی (classical بدین = 'of/with this' + نیکوی 'good/excellent') modifies the preceding noun: 'speech so excellent.'
segments.s159-g208: فزاید is a present/future-oriented verb 'it increases' or 'it adds to'. همی is a classical particle here meaning 'indeed/truly' or continuous emphasis. مغز is used figuratively as 'mind/intellect', and کاین is a relative phrase 'that which/this which'. بشنوی is 2nd-person subjunctive/optative 'you hear' (literally 'when/if you hear it'). Together: 'It indeed increases the mind when you hear this.'
segments.s159-g257: بران is a prepositional/locative particle meaning "upon that" and introduces the location; تخت سودابه is a noun + proper name construction meaning "Sudabeh's throne" (noun before name is common in poetic Persian). ماه‌روی is a compound-like descriptive phrase where ماه (literally "moon" / figuratively "beautiful one") modifies روی ("face"); together it means "moon-faced" or "beautiful-faced." The whole segment supplies the subject/location of the clause.
segments.s159-g258: بسان is a simile marker meaning "like/as." بهشتی here is adjectival, "heavenly / from paradise." پر از is an idiom meaning "full of" (پر followed by از + noun); رنگ و بوی are coordinated nouns "color and scent." This segment is a simileial description that modifies the moon‑faced figure introduced before.
segments.s159-g259: نشسته is a past participle/adjectival predicate meaning "seated / sitting" and here describes her posture. چو introduces a simile "as/like," followed by تابان سهیل یمن, an epithet: تابان "shining" qualifies سهیل (the star Canopus) and یمن here is used in the classical sense "of good omen/auspicious," so the phrase reads "like the shining Canopus of good omen."
segments.s159-g260: سر is "head," followed by جعد زلفش where جعد "curl" modifies زلف ("lock/tress of hair") and the enclitic ش marks possession "her." سراسر is an adverb meaning "throughout/entirely," and شکن is a nominal epithet "breaker" (here figuratively "heart‑breaker"). The noun phrase describes her hair as wholly a breaker — a compact, poetic predicative description.
segments.s159-g261: یکی here is the indefinite "one / a certain (one)" introducing an element of the scene. تاج is the head‑noun "crown," بر سر is the prepositional phrase "on the head," and نهاده is the past participle "placed/put." بلند is an adjective "tall/lofty" qualifying the crown. Word order is typical poetic Persian: indefinite + noun + prepositional phrase + participle + adjective.
segments.s159-g262: فرو is an adverbial prefix/particle meaning "downwards," modifying هشته (a cluster/lock of hair). تا marks extent "as far as/to," پای is "foot," and مشکین modifies کمند: مشکین here is adjectival ("musk‑scented / musk‑colored / dark") and کمند is "noose/snare." Together the segment pictures a lock of hair hanging down to her foot like a dark/musky snare. The modifiers follow the nouns in typical Persian order.
segments.s159-g263: پرستار is a noun meaning "attendant/caretaker" here (not the religious sense). نعلین زرین is a noun + adjective "golden sandals," and بدست is an adjectival/phrastic modifier "in the hand / holding." The whole noun phrase describes an attendant who has golden sandals in hand.
segments.s159-g264: به پای is a locative phrase "at (her) feet" (به meaning "at/to" here), ایستاده is a participle/predicate "standing," and سرافگنده پست are two adjectival predicates in sequence: سرافگنده "ashamed/humbled" and پست "low/mean/lowly." They together qualify the attendant's demeanour. The order places the location and posture before the emotional/state adjectives, typical in Persian description.
segments.s159-g265: چو is a temporal conjunction meaning 'when' placed after the subject; the clause follows classical SOV order with the subject سیاوش first and the verb برفت (an archaic/poetic past of رفتن) at the end. از پیش پرده is a prepositional phrase 'from/in front of the curtain' modifying the motion verb.
segments.s159-g266: فرود آمد is the compound verb فرود آمدن in the past tense meaning 'descended' or 'alighted' (فرود = descent + آمد = came). از تخت سودابه is a from‑phrase 'from the throne of Sudabeh' where تخت is 'throne' and سودابه a proper name; تفت here is an attached word whose sense is unclear/archaic in the provided inventory and likely descriptive or proper.
segments.s159-g267: بیامد is a past tense verb 'came' (here with iterative nuance when combined with خرامان), and خرامان is an adverb/adjectival adverb meaning 'with a proud/stately gait.' The coordinating و links the next predicate; بردش followed by نماز is an idiomatic sequence meaning 'took up/took on (something) — here the object نماز (prayer),' but بردش in the provided senses list does not match this verbal use, so its precise lexical sense is outside the given options.
segments.s159-g268: به بر در گرفتش is a compact expression: به بر = 'into/onto his bosom/arms' (به + بر marking direction/position), در here is part of the verbal phrase intensifying the grip, and گرفتش is the past tense 'he seized/held him' (گرفت + ـش object clitic). زمانی دراز is an adverbial phrase 'for a long time' (زمانی = 'a while', دراز = 'long'), placed after the verb as is normal in Persian.
segments.s159-g269: همی before the verb gives a continuous or iterative sense ('kept on, was continually'), so همی ... ببوسید = 'he kept kissing.' چشم و رویش lists direct objects in the order 'eyes and his/her face' with the possessive clitic ش attached to روی. دیر here begins the following temporal clause and means 'long' (as in 'it was not long...').
segments.s159-g270: نیامد here is used impersonally with ز دیدار ... سیر to mean 'it did not come (to be) [satiated]' — idiomatically 'was not sated.' ز introduces the cause/source 'from the sight/meeting (دیدار) of that king (آن شاه).' سیر is a predicate nominal meaning 'sated/satisfaction.' Word order is standard: verb then prepositional cause phrase and predicate nominal.
segments.s159-g271: همی again gives iterative sense 'kept saying.' گفت here functions as a simple past reporting speech (the provided sense list lacked the straightforward 'said' sense, so that item is marked outside the given options). صد ره is a classical poetic quantifier 'a hundred times' (ره used as 'occasion/instance' in this idiom), ز یزدان سپاس = 'thanks from God' i.e. 'thanks to God' or 'thanks be to God' with ز marking source/benefactor and سپاس 'thanks/praise' as the object.
segments.s159-g272: نیایش کنم is the present/subjunctive-like construction 'I will offer/pray' (نیایش 'prayer' + کنم 'I do/make' used to express performing the act). روز و شب 'day and night' is an adverbial pair; بر سه پاس is a prepositional phrase where بر = 'in/on/at' and سه پاس = 'three watches/turns' (پاس = watch/guard); the whole phrase specifies frequency/timing of the prayers.
segments.s159-g177: This is a temporal subordinate phrase: 'سیاوش' (Siavash) is the subject, and 'چو' is the archaic conjunction 'when' introducing the clause; 'بشنید' is a past tense verb 'heard'. 'گفتار شاه' is a genitive/possessive noun phrase 'the king's speech' (noun + possessor), placed after the verb as is common in classical Persian.
segments.s159-g178: 'همی کرد' here is a classical/periphrastic marker showing continued or habitual action ('he kept / was doing'); 'خیره' is an adverb/adjectival participle meaning 'staring' or 'fixedly', and 'بدو در نگاه' = 'at him in gaze' (بدو = 'to him', در نگاه = 'in the look/gaze'). Word order places the descriptive element before the light verb 'کرد'.
segments.s159-g179: 'زمانی' is an adverbial phrase here meaning 'for a while' (a temporal adverb). 'همی' again marks ongoing or emphatic action, and 'با دل اندیشه کرد' literally 'he thought with his heart' — 'با' marks the means/instrument ('with'), 'دل' is the inner seat of thought, and 'اندیشه کرد' is a past-tense compound meaning 'he pondered/reflecte d'.
segments.s159-g180: 'بکوشید' is a past tense verb meaning 'he strove/endeavoured'. 'تا' introduces a purpose clause ('so that / in order that'), and the subordinate verb 'بشوید' is jussive/subjunctive in sense 'might become / be made' (here 'be washed/cleansed'). 'ز گرد' is an ablative phrase 'from dust' (ز = 'from', گرد = 'dust'), describing what the heart should be cleansed of.
segments.s159-g181: 'گمانی' is 'a thought/supposition' (the mental event). 'چنان' intensifies it ('such/so'), and 'برد' here is a past-tense verb meaning 'seized/came upon' (classical usage 'a thought took him'). 'کاو را' is an archaic/clitic form (equivalent to 'که او را' or 'that he') followed by 'پدر' ('father') as the topical/object referent; 'را' is the definite object marker attached to that phrase.
segments.s159-g182: 'پژوهد همی' is a verbal phrase meaning 'he investigates / inquires' with 'همی' again indicating ongoing action. 'تا' introduces the indirect question/purpose 'to find out', and 'چه دارد به سر' is an idiomatic clause 'what he has in his head' = 'what he is thinking/what his intention is' (چه = 'what', دارد = 'has', به سر = 'in the head/mind').
segments.s159-g183: 'که' is the relative/conjunction linking this descriptive clause to the preceding noun ('his father' implied). 'بسیاردان است' is the predicate 'is very learned' ('بسیاردان' = learned/expert; 'است' = is). The coordinated phrase 'و چیره زبان' follows: 'چیره' (skilled/dominant) modifies 'زبان' (here 'speech' or 'eloquence'), giving 'eloquent' or 'skilled of tongue'.
segments.s159-g184: This is a simple coordination of character traits: 'هشیوار' (alert, vigilant) and 'بینادل' and 'بدگمان'. The conjunction 'و' links the adjectives; these are descriptive epithets of the same antecedent. (Note: 'بینادل' is an older/poetic adjective; the list provided treats it as a rare/uncertain lemma.)
segments.s159-g105: A noun phrase built around بزرگان ایران ("the great men of Iran"); همه follows to quantify ('all of them'). با نثار is an accompanying prepositional phrase ('with offerings'). Word order (noun + modifier + quantifier + accompaniment) is typical of elevated/poetic Persian.
segments.s159-g106: برفتند is a past-tense third-person plural verb ('they went/approached'); شادان is an adjectival/adverbial complement ('joyful') placed after the verb for poetic effect; بر + شهریار gives the direction/goal ('toward/to the king').
segments.s159-g107: ز introduces the source/cause ('from, because of') and takes فر سیاوش as its object ('Siavash's glory'); فرو ماندند is a compound with فرو (down/under) + ماندند (past plural 'remained') meaning 'they remained/ stood down/ were brought low' — here 'were humbled/overwhelmed by'.
segments.s159-g108: This clause reports what they uttered: بدادار and برآفرین are vocative/epithet words invoked together, and خواندند is the past plural verb 'they called/uttered' (here 'they invoked/uttered aloud'). In poetic usage such nominal epithets can be invoked in sequence; خواندند indicates they spoke or chanted those names/phrases.
segments.s159-g109: بفرمود is an honorific past-tense verb 'he commanded/ordered'. تا introduces the subordinate clause of purpose/result ('so that / in order that'). پیشش is a locative 'before him / in his presence' and ایرانیان is the subject of the subordinate clause ('the Iranians').
segments.s159-g110: ببستند is past third-person plural 'they closed/arrayed/formed' (here: they 'closed up' or 'formed' ranks). گردان لشکر is a genitive NP 'the division/troop of the army' and میان follows to indicate location 'in the middle.' The verb governs the whole phrase as its object/complement.
segments.s159-g111: A series of prepositional phrases introduced by به ('to/at/into') listing places: به کاخ و به باغ و به میدان — 'to/at the palace and the garden and the field'. اوی is the possessive pronoun 'his,' attached after میدان to mark ownership ('his palace/garden/field'). The repetition of به is a poetic distributive construction.
segments.s159-g112: جهانی here functions adverbially ('throughout/all over' or 'universal'). به شادی is a prepositional phrase 'into/with joy'. نهادند is the past plural of نهادن ('they placed/caused'); combined with روی ('face') the phrase نهادند روی is a poetic idiom meaning 'they turned/set (their) face (toward) — i.e. they caused things to be joyful' or more simply 'they filled with joy.'
segments.s159-g329: که introduces a content clause meaning 'that'. ماند is a third-person singular verb (classical/simple form) meaning 'remain' or 'last' (here prospective/resultative). ز (poetic for از) = 'from/of'; the phrase ز تو نام من یادگار literally orders elements 'from you / my name / a memorial' — Persian allows this noun order and یادگار is the predicate 'a memorial (will remain)'.
segments.s159-g330: ز تخم تو = 'from your seed/lineage' (ز = 'from'). آید is the simple present/potential 'comes' (classical Persian uses this for future/inevitable events). یکی شهریار = 'one king' (یکی = numeral 'one'; شهریار = 'king/sovereign').
segments.s159-g331: چنان ... کز introduces a result clause 'so that / such that'. کز (archaic contraction of که از) here means 'because of' or 'from' in the sense 'by/through you': 'کز تو من گشته‌ام تازه روی' = 'because of you I have become fresh-faced'. گشته‌ام is the perfect/resultative 'I have become'. تازه روی is a compound adjective 'fresh-faced/renewed in appearance' placed after the verb.
segments.s159-g332: تو is the vocative/subject 'you'. دل برگشایی is a second-person present/imperative form (برگشایی = 'you open' in classical ending -ی) meaning 'open your heart'. به دیدار اوی = 'to the sight/meeting of him' = 'to behold him' (اوی is an older form of او meaning 'him').
segments.s159-g333: چنین یافتم = 'thus I found' (یافتم = 1st-person past of یافتن). اخترت را نشان = 'a sign of your star' — اخترt (اختر + possessive ت) 'your star', را is the definite/object marker, and نشان is the noun 'sign' as the object of یافتم.
segments.s159-g334: ز گفت here uses گفت as a noun 'the saying/account' (not the past-tense verb 'said'), so ز گفت = 'from the account/saying'. ستاره شمر موبدان should be read as an appositive/genitive grouping: 'the star “Shamr” (as reported) of the mobeds' — i.e. the mobeds' account concerning the star called Shamr. Word order is elliptical/poetic and omits explicit particles.
segments.s159-g335: که introduces a subordinate clause 'that'. از پشت تو = literally 'from your back' but idiomatically 'from your descendants/lineage' (پشت used figuratively). شهریاری بود = 'there was/would be a sovereignty/sovereign' (بود is past-tense copula here used in a prophetic/nominal sense).
segments.s159-g336: که ... یادگاری بود = a parallel clause 'that in the world there would be a memorial'. اندر جهان = 'in the world' (اندر = archaic/prepositional 'in/within'), یادگاری = 'a memento/lasting remembrance', بود = 'was/would be' (past/copular used here to state the existence of that memorial).
segments.s159-g313: هم begins the clause as an adverb meaning “also/too.” از تخم خویشش is a possessive genitive phrase: از + تخم (seed, i.e. lineage) + خویشش (his own) = “from his stock.” یکی زن دهم uses the verb دهم (classical present/subjunctive form of دادن, “I give”/“I will give”) with the direct object زن preceded by the numeral یکی; the subject is omitted and understood as the speaker (“I will give one woman/wife”).
segments.s159-g314: نه is a negation (here coordinating: “nor/not”), followed by another از + noun phrase. نامداران is the plural “the renowned/illustrious.” برزن stands for a public/open place (here adverbially, “in public/among the people”). دهم is again the verb “I give,” so the clause means “I will not give (her) to the famous in public.”
segments.s159-g315: که is the relative pronoun “who/that” introducing a relative clause. فرزند آرد uses آرد (classical present/subjunctive of آوردن here meaning “brings/shall bring”) with فرزند as object: “brings a child.” ورا is the enclitic לה/به او meaning “to him,” and در جهان simply locates the event “in the world.” The verb is in the classical present/subjunctive form but functions here as a prophetic/relative predicate.
segments.s159-g316: به دیدار او is a purposive phrase: به + دیدار (meeting/seeing) + او “to see/meet him.” در میان مهان is a locative phrase meaning “among the guests” (در میان = among, مهان = guests). Together the phrase expresses the intended occasion or setting of the meeting.
segments.s159-g317: مرا here functions as a possessive/existential construction (literally “to me”/“for me there are”), with را shown separately as the classical object/topic marker; together with دخترانند (دختران + اند) it means “I have daughters” or “to me there are daughters.” مانند تو is a simile “like you.” Word order is topical/existential: indirect object/possessive first, predicate after.
segments.s159-g318: ز تخم تو is a genitive phrase using poetic ز (equivalent to از) meaning “from your seed/stock.” و links a second attribute پاک پیوند تو: پاک (adjective “pure”) modifies پیوند (bond/kinship), and the final تو marks possession: “and of your pure kinship.” The whole phrase specifies lineage and purity of descent.
segments.s159-g319: گر is the conditional “if.” از تخم is “from the seed/stock.” کی here means “which/who” in a rhetorical sense (“someone like”), so کی آرش و کی پشین lists examples — “(someone) like Arash or like the former (one).” This is a protasis naming possible descendants.
segments.s159-g320: بخواهد is the classical present/subjunctive of خواستن used here as a modal/future-like “should wish/if he will,” introducing the apodosis. به شادی کند is a purposive/optative phrase: به شادی “with joy” + کند (present of کردن, “do/make”) = “may he make/express joy.” آفرین at the end is the exclamation/noun “acclamation/bravo” — the thing he utters in joy. The clause expresses allowance or approval in the hypothetical case.
segments.s159-g321: بدو گفت = “he said to him.” بدو is the prepositional pronoun ‘to him/unto it’ placed before the verb (typical Persian order in poetry). گفت is the past tense verb ‘said.’ این خود بکام منست is a small clause: این خود = “this very (thing),” بکام = an adjective/compound meaning “to the taste/pleasing,” and منست is an archaic/poetic copula form (contracted) meaning “is for me / is mine” (literally b-kām man-ast). So the whole segment reads ‘He said to him: This very thing is pleasing to me.’
segments.s159-g322: بزرگی = subject ‘greatness/grandeur.’ به فرجام = a prepositional phrase ‘to/at the end/outcome,’ where به marks relation (‘as/to the outcome of’). نام = ‘name’ but here best rendered as ‘reputation/fame.’ منست, again the archaic copula, states existence/attribution (‘is’/‘belongs’). Word order is subject–prepositional phrase–copula, so ‘Greatness is the outcome of my name.’
segments.s159-g323: سیاوش is the proper name (subject). به شبگیر شد is a poetic phrasing: به + شبگیر (night-visitor/nocturnal) + شد (‘became’/‘went’), meaning he came/appeared by night or took on the role of a night-visitor. نزد شاه = ‘into the presence of the king / to the king.’ The clause therefore means ‘Siyavash, coming by night, went to the king.’
segments.s159-g324: همی gives a continuous or iterative sense (‘continually/kept on’). آفرین here is the exclamation/acclamation ‘praise / bravo,’ and خواند is the past tense of the verb ‘to utter/recite/sing,’ so آفرین خواند = ‘he uttered acclamations.’ بر marks the target (‘upon’), and تاج و گاه = ‘the crown and the (royal) seat/occasion’ (گاه in older/poetic usage can imply the seat/throne or occasion). Altogether: ‘he repeatedly cried “bravo” over the crown and throne.’
segments.s159-g325: پدر = ‘the father’ (subject). با پسر = ‘with the son’ (indicating accompaniment or the addressee). راز گفتن = the infinitive/nominalized verb phrase ‘telling secrets’ (راز = secret; گفتن = to tell). گرفت is past ‘took’ used idiomatically with an infinitive here to mean ‘took to / began (doing)’. So ‘The father, with the son, took to telling secrets’ (i.e. began to tell secrets to his son).
segments.s159-g326: ز = poetic form of از ‘from.’ بیگانه مردم = ‘strange/foreign people’ or ‘strangers.’ نهفتن = infinitive ‘to hide / to keep secret.’ گرفت again = ‘took’ / ‘began.’ The phrase means ‘he began hiding [them] from strangers’ (the object ‘them’ = the secrets is implied).
segments.s159-g327: همی گفت is a habitual or iterative past ‘he kept saying’/‘he said repeatedly.’ کز is a contracted conjunction (poetic for که از/که ز) meaning ‘that/from which’ or introducing the content of what is said. کردگار جهان = ‘the Maker/Creator of the world’ (an epithet for God). So this segment introduces the reported content: ‘he said that, from the Creator of the world…’
segments.s159-g328: یکی = ‘one / a single / a certain’ (quantifier). آرزو دارم = literal present ‘I have a wish’ (in reported speech here; Persian poetry often preserves present form in narration). اندر نهان = adverbial phrase ‘in secret / secretly.’ Together: ‘I have one secret wish,’ i.e. ‘he said that he had one desire in secret.’
segments.s159-g169: پس is a discourse adverb meaning 'so/then' that opens or continues the statement. پرده من is a possessive phrase ('پرده' + 'من' = 'my veil' / 'my veiled one'); ترا is the dative (ت + را) meaning 'to you'; خواهرست is the predicative 'خواهر + است' contracted in poetic language (ست) meaning 'is a sister'. The clause therefore states a relation: 'As for (my) veil/my veiled one, it is a sister to you.'
segments.s159-g170: چو introduces a comparison 'as/just as'. سودابه is a proper name; خود is an intensifier 'herself'. مهربان is the adjective 'kind, loving' and مادرست is again a noun + archaic contracted copula ('مادر + است') meaning 'is a mother' or 'is motherly'. The whole segment draws a parallel: 'just as Sūdābeh herself is a loving mother.'
segments.s159-g171: ترا (ت + را) is the object 'you' in dative form. پاک یزدان is a noun phrase 'the pure God' (پاک modifying یزدان). چنان is an adverb 'so/such', and آفرید is the past tense 'created/made'. Word order is classical Persian: object-marker + creator phrase + manner + verb — 'God created you thus.'
segments.s159-g172: که introduces a result or explanatory clause ('such that/so that'). مهر is 'love/affection', آوَرد is past 'brought' / 'caused to come upon'. بر تو is the prepositional phrase 'upon you'. هرکت بدید in the manuscript reading is irregular here (contextually => 'هر که بدید' = 'whoever saw'); بدید is the past 'saw'. The intended meaning is resultative: 'so that whoever saw you, love came upon him.'
segments.s159-g173: به ویژه is a fixed expression 'especially' (به + ویژه). که here gives the reason or explanation ('because/since'). پیوسته is an adverb 'constantly/continuously', خون in this context means 'blood' in the sense of 'bloodshed'. بود is the past copula 'was'. Together: 'especially because blood was constantly present / there was constant bloodshed.'
segments.s159-g174: چو at the start is 'when'. از دور is the adverbial 'from afar/from a distance'. بیند is the classical-subjunctive/present form of 'to see' used here in the narrative sense 'he saw'. ترا (ت + را) is 'you' (object), and the final چون بود is an exclamatory tag 'how he was' or 'what a state he was in' (چون = 'how', بود = 'was'). The clause reads roughly 'when he saw you from afar, how he was.'
segments.s159-g175: پس is 'so/then'. پرده پوشیدگان is a compound: پرده (veil/curtain) + پوشیدگان (the participial plural 'those covered/veiled' from پوشیدن) — 'the veiled ones' or 'those behind the curtain'. را is the object marker, and ببین is the imperative 'look' / 'see'. The clause is an imperative: 'Then look at the veiled ones.'
segments.s159-g176: زمانی بمان is an imperative request 'stay/remain for a time' (زمانی = 'for a while'). تا introduces a purposive/result clause 'until/so that', and کنند is the subjunctive 3rd-plural of كردن 'they do/make'. آفرین here is a nominal interjection 'praise/bravo' (i.e. 'crying out praise'). The sequence means 'stay a moment until they offer/utter praise.' Note the subjunctive конند after تا expresses the anticipated action.
segments.s159-g217: یریِ 'یکی' here means 'a certain' and introduces an indefinite/identifying noun phrase. 'مرد بد نام' is an accusative/nominal phrase meaning 'an ill‑famed man' (بدنام = infamous). 'او' is an appositive pronoun pointing back to that man, followed by the title/name 'هیربد' (so: 'he, the Hirbad'). Word order is typical classical Persian: determiner + noun + epithet, then an appositive pronoun and the name/title.
segments.s159-g218: 'زدوده' is a perfective/past participle meaning 'cleansed / rid (of)'. It predicates over the following coordinated parts 'دل و مغز و رایش' (heart/soul, brain, and his judgment), and the preposition 'ز' + 'بد' ('from/because of bad') marks what they were rid of: evil or badness. Note 'رایش' already contains the 3rd‑person possessive element ('his') and so applies personally to that man.
segments.s159-g219: The relative/causal particle 'که' introduces the clause describing the man. 'بتخانه را' is the definite object/topic 'the idol‑house' (را marking definite object). 'هیچ' here means 'anything/nothing', and 'نگذاشتی' is the past negative 'did not leave / left no (thing)'. The verb refers back to the previously mentioned man (classical past form).
segments.s159-g220: This clause gives the complementary detail: 'کلید' (the key) + locative 'در پرده او' ('in its/that curtain') followed by the past verb 'داشتی' ('had'). Word order places the thing possessed first, the location phrase next, and the verb at the end as is common in classical Persian; 'او' refers back to the idol‑house (poetic use of the pronoun for an object).
segments.s159-g221: 'سپهدار ایران' is the subject phrase 'the commander of Iran'. 'به فرزانه گفت' is 'said to the sage' with the indirect object marked by 'به' and the verb 'گفت' at the end. (The verb form is the narrative past.)
segments.s159-g222: The subordinating 'که' + temporal 'چون' introduce the clause 'when he draws...'. 'برکشد' is the classical present/subjunctive-form meaning 'draws (up)/draws (a weapon)'. 'تیغ هور' is the object 'the sword of Hūr' (Hūr treated as a proper name), and 'از نهفت' is a poetic locative/adverbial 'out of concealment/hiddenness'. Word order places the object before the verb as is common in classical narrative clauses.
segments.s159-g223: The prepositional phrase 'به پیش سیاوش' = 'to the presence of Siawash'. 'همی' is the classical progressive/emphatic prefix and here accompanies the verb form 'رو' (an archaic/epic form of 'to go'), giving an instruction 'go (now/go at once)'. 'بهوش' is an adverbial/adjunct meaning 'awake/alert/attentive' — 'go to Siawash, be alert.' Note: 'رو' appears here as a verbal form; its dictionary senses listed as a noun do not capture this verbal use.
segments.s159-g224: 'نگر' is an imperative 'look!/see!'. 'تا' here introduces the purpose/result clause 'so that/and see what...' and 'چه فرماید' = 'what he may say' (فرماید is the polite/classical present/sjunctive of 'to say/command'). 'آن دار گوش' is an epithet: 'that (آن) دارِ گوش' literally 'possessor of ear' — i.e. 'the one who listens / the attentive lord' (gosh = attention/ear). The noun 'دار' is used as 'possessor/holder' in this classical phrase (sense mapping for 'دار' is not exact in the given list).
segments.s159-g345: نباید is an impersonal/modal negative meaning 'it should not / must not (be that ...)'. که introduces the subordinate clause 'that Soudabeh hears this'. سودابه is the proper name (subject). این is the demonstrative 'this' (the thing just said). بشنود is a 3rd-person present/subjunctive form of شنیدن 'to hear' used here to express the possibility ('hear') within the subordinate clause.
segments.s159-g346: دگرگونه is an adverb 'otherwise / differently' modifying the verb that follows. گوید here functions as a 3rd-person present/optative of گفتن 'to say' — 'she may say / will say otherwise'. بدین should be read as the fused prepositional phrase 'بدین' ('by/with this' or 'to this'), formed here from ب + دین; نگرود is the verb (poetically formed) meaning 'adopt / take (on)' (here with a negative or prohibitive sense in the overall clause), so the clause says she may 'say otherwise and adopt this'.
segments.s159-g347: به + سودابه front the clause as a dativelike/experiencer phrase 'to/for Soudabeh' (here: 'as for Soudabeh'). زین‌گونه گفتار is the noun phrase 'speech of this kind / such speech' (زین‌گونه = 'thus/like this' modifying گفتار = 'speech'). نیست negates the existence of that speech with respect to her: 'There is no such speech for Soudabeh' (i.e. she does not have / should not hear such talk).
segments.s159-g348: مرا در این line is the topical/affected phrase 'مرا' (me), where م + را is the clitic split of the object 'mearā' = 'me'. در شبستان او is a locative prepositional phrase 'in her bedchamber' (شبستان = inner chamber/bedchamber; او = 'her'). کار نیست is an existential negation 'there is no business/affair' — together: 'I have no business in her bedchamber.' Word order places the experiencer/topic (مرا) up front for emphasis.
segments.s159-g349: ز is the poetic preposition 'from / because of' indicating cause. گفت here stands for 'the saying / words' (a noun derived from گفتن) and combines with سیاوش to form 'the words of Siawash' (a genitive/attributive noun phrase). بخندید is the 3rd-person past/ perfect of خندیدن 'to laugh' and شاه is the subject placed after the verb (typical of older/poetic Persian): 'the king laughed at Siawash's words.'
segments.s159-g350: نه آگاه is a negated predicate 'not aware' (نه negates آگاه 'aware'). بد here qualifies (literally 'bad' / 'ill'), describing the state — in context 'ill-informed' or 'unaware in a bad way'. ز introduces a relation 'about/from' and is followed by the phrase آب در زیر کاه 'water under the straw' (آب = water; در زیر کاه = 'under the straw'). The whole compressed poetic clause indicates the king was unaware (and thus poorly informed) regarding the matter described as 'water under the straw' (a figurative/poetic phrase).
segments.s159-g351: گزین is an imperative/infinitive root meaning 'choose / select' used here as an instruction. تو باید places the obligation on 'you' ('you must'). بدو گفت is the phrase 'say to him' (بدو = 'to him/unto him', گفت = 'say' or 'tell'), and زن at the end is the object or specification 'a woman' (either what to choose or the vocative). The clause is an instruction: 'You must tell him to choose (a) woman.'
segments.s159-g352: ازو is 'from/of her' (literally 'from her' or 'concerning her'). هیچ is the negator 'nothing / not at all'. مندیش is a poetic/archaic verb-form meaning roughly 'do not think / do not let remain / do not let be present' (here a prohibitive/inhibitory sense). وز (و + ز) links or reinforces the prepositional sense 'and/from' introducing the next noun انجمن 'assembly / council'. Together the clause means 'let nothing of her be in (or come to) the assembly' — an instruction to exclude her from the council.
segments.s159-g337: A short imperative clause. کنون ('now') is an adverb fronted for emphasis; از بزرگان ('from the great ones') is a prepositional phrase showing the set being chosen from; یکی ('one') is the numeral/indefinite head and برگزین is an imperative (2nd person singular) 'choose!'. Word order is adverb — prepositional phrase — numeral — imperative, typical of poetic Persian where imperatives can conclude the clause.
segments.s159-g338: Two coordinated imperatives/commands. نگه کن is an imperative formed from نگه (root notion 'hold/watch') + کن (imperative 'do'), meaning 'keep watch/see'. پس پرده is a noun phrase where پس is the postpositive/poetic preposition 'after/behind' and پرده 'curtain' — together 'behind the curtain'. کی is the interrogative 'who' asking about the person located there; پشین is an archaic modifier ('former/before') used poetically here to indicate the person located just 'before/behind' the curtain. The sequence gives a single instruction: 'look and find who is behind the curtain.'
segments.s159-g339: A locative/identificational clause. به خان places the action 'at the khan's (court)' or 'to the khan'; کی here functions as an interrogative/relative 'who' or 'whom' in older usage but in context links the name, while آرش همان نیز هست is a verbless/nominal phrase where همان ('that very / himself') + نیز ('also') + هست ('is') states that Arash is likewise present. Word order (locative — relative/identifier — name — resumptive demonstrative — copula) is typical of epic diction.
segments.s159-g340: An imperative / hortative instruction. ز هر سو ('from every side') is a prepositional phrase indicating surroundingly; بیارای here is a verb in the imperative/hortative sense 'array/prepare/ornament' (poetic use); و connects to the following verb; بپساو is an archaic verb meaning 'then follow/bring forth' (imperative form), and دست ('hand') is the object — likely meaning 'bring forward his hand' or 'place his hand'. The clause orders that he be dressed/arranged on all sides and that his hand be presented.
segments.s159-g341: Reported speech introduced by a dative-like phrase. بدو is an enclitic/adverbial meaning 'to him' (i.e., 'he said to him'); گفت here introduces direct speech ('he said') — note the editor's lemma list shows noun senses but in the line it is the past-tense verb 'said'. من شاه را بنده‌ام is the quoted utterance: من 'I' + شاه را ('the king' marked with the object/possessive marker را used here in an idiomatic possessive sense) + بنده‌ام (بنده + ـ‌ام, first person singular copula suffix) = 'I am the king's servant.'
segments.s159-g342: Continuation of the reported speech describing the speaker's stance. به فرمان ('at/by the command') + و ('and') + رایش ('his counsel/judgment' — رایش = رای + ـش 'his') form two prepositional objects; سرافگنده‌ام is an adjective with the first-person suffix 'ـام' meaning 'I am abashed/ashamed/disgraced'. Grammatically this is a verbless predicate in which the speaker humbles himself before the king's orders and opinions.
segments.s159-g343: A conditional/free-relative clause. هرآن کس که ('whenever/whomsoever person that') introduces a free relative; او برگزیند is a present/subjunctive third-person verb 'he selects/chooses' (برگزیند here expresses potential/selected action); رواست is composed of روا ('fitting/permissible') + ست (contracted form of 'است', 'is') giving 'it is fitting/permissible'. The whole reads 'Whomever he chooses, it is fitting.'
segments.s159-g344: A general statement about authority. جهاندار is a compound title 'world-holder/sovereign' (i.e., the king); بربندگان is بر + بندگان 'over the servants/subjects' (the prefix بر = 'over/upon' plus بندگان 'servants'); پادشاست is another form of the copula + predicate meaning 'is king/sovereign' or 'is the ruler'. Together: 'The sovereign is (lord) over the servants,' underlining that the ruler's choice is authoritative.
